html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 閱微草堂筆記 - Title of the work; can be translated as *The Studio of Reading the Subtle: Notes*. *閱微* (yue wei) means 'to read the subtle' or 'to observe the minute', *草堂* (cao tang) refers to a thatched cottage or study, often used as a humble name for a scholar's residence, and *筆記* (bi ji) means 'notes' or 'jottings'.
  • 滦陽消夏錄 - Title of the first section; can be translated as *Pianyang Summer Record* or *Records of Summer in Pianyang*. *滦陽* (Pianyang) is a place name, likely referring to a region in northern China, and *消夏錄* (xiao xia lu) means 'summer record' or 'summer notes'.
  • 如是我聞 - Title of the second section; can be translated as *Thus Have I Heard* or *As I Have Heard*. This is a common Buddhist phrase, often used in sutras to indicate that the speaker is recounting something they have heard from a spiritual teacher.
  • 槐西雜誌 - Title of the third section; can be translated as *Miscellaneous Notes from the West Willow* or *Miscellaneous Records of the West Willow*. *槐西* (huaixi) likely refers to a place or a symbolic reference, and *雜誌* (zazhi) means 'miscellaneous notes' or 'miscellaneous records'.
  • 姑妄聽之 - Title of the fourth section; can be translated as *Take It with a Grain of Salt* or *Listen to It Carefully*. This phrase suggests that the reader should take the content with caution or skepticism.
  • 滦陽續錄 - Title of the fifth section; can be translated as *Continuation of the Pianyang Record* or *Further Records of Pianyang*. *續錄* (xulu) means 'continuation' or 'further notes'.
  • 卷一滦陽消夏錄（一）四十七則 - Structure typical of classical Chinese texts; indicates the first part of the first volume, containing 47 entries. *則* (ze) can be translated as 'entries' or 'items'.
  • 如是我聞（一）六十三則 - Indicates the first part of the second section, containing 63 entries.
  • 滦陽續錄（一）二十九則 - Indicates the first part of the fifth section, containing 29 entries.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 紀昀 - The author of *The Studio of Reading the Subtle*. He was a prominent scholar, official, and literary figure during the Qing dynasty, best known for his role in compiling the *Siku Quanshu* (Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature), the largest collection of Chinese literature ever assembled. Also known by his courtesy name *曉嵐* (Xiaolan) and his posthumous title *文達公* (Wenda Gong).
  • 清 - Refers to the Qing dynasty (1644–1912), the last imperial dynasty of China. The Qing period was marked by significant cultural and literary developments, and *The Studio of Reading the Subtle* is a product of this era.
  • 滦陽 - Likely refers to a region in northern China, possibly near the Hebei province, where the author may have spent time or where the events described in the work took place.
  • 消夏 - Literally means 'to dissipate summer heat' or 'to spend the summer', often used in the context of leisurely activities during the hot season.
  • 如是我聞 - A Buddhist phrase that appears in many sutras, indicating that the speaker is recounting something they have heard from a spiritual teacher. This phrase is used in the second section of the work, suggesting a connection to Buddhist thought or storytelling traditions.
  • 姑妄聽之 - A phrase that suggests the reader should take the content with caution, implying that the stories or accounts in this section may be uncertain or speculative.

Proper Nouns

  • 紀昀 - The author of the work, also known by his courtesy name *曉嵐* (Xiaolan) and his posthumous title *文達公* (Wenda Gong).
  • 清 - The Qing dynasty, the last imperial dynasty of China.
  • 滦陽 - A place name, likely referring to a region in northern China.
  • 如是我聞 - A Buddhist phrase, often used in sutras to indicate that the speaker is recounting something they have heard from a spiritual teacher.
  • 姑妄聽之 - A phrase suggesting that the reader should take the content with caution.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 千生心力坐销磨 - Poetic expression meaning 'a thousand lifetimes of effort are worn away,' emphasizing the immense effort and time spent on scholarly pursuits.
  • 纸上烟云过眼多 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the words on paper pass by like fleeting clouds,' suggesting the ephemeral nature of written works.
  • 拟筑书仓今老矣 - Phrase meaning 'I planned to build a library, but now I am old,' expressing regret or resignation about unfulfilled ambitions.
  • 只应说鬼以东坡 - Colloquial expression meaning 'one should only talk about ghosts as Su Dongpo did,' referring to Su Shi's (Su Dongpo) literary style, which often included ghost stories.
  • 前因后果验无差 - Phrase meaning 'the causes and effects are verified without error,' emphasizing the accuracy and consistency of historical records.
  • 琐记搜罗鬼一车 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'collecting trivial notes like a cart full of ghosts,' suggesting the accumulation of numerous, perhaps insignificant, details.
  • 传语洛闰门弟子 - Phrase meaning 'tell the disciples of the Luo gate,' referring to the followers or students of a particular school or master.
  • 稗官原不入儒家 - Phrase meaning 'minor officials were originally not part of the Confucian tradition,' highlighting the distinction between minor officials and the Confucian scholarly class.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 纪昀 - Reference to Ji Yun, a prominent Chinese scholar and official during the Qing dynasty, known for his contributions to literature and scholarship.
  • 馆阁 - Refers to the imperial library or the scholarly institution where Ji Yun worked.
  • 鸿文巨制 - Term meaning 'grand literary works,' referring to significant and influential writings.
  • 东坡 - Reference to Su Shi (Su Dongpo), a renowned Song dynasty poet and statesman known for his literary style and ghost stories.
  • 洛闰门弟子 - Refers to the disciples or followers of a particular school or master, likely in the context of Confucian scholarship.
  • 稗官 - Refers to minor officials or low-ranking bureaucrats, often in contrast to the Confucian scholarly class.

Proper Nouns

  • 纪昀 - Ji Yun, a prominent Qing dynasty scholar and official.
  • 东坡 - Su Shi (Su Dongpo), a renowned Song dynasty poet and statesman.
  • 洛闰门弟子 - The disciples or followers of a particular school or master, likely in the context of Confucian scholarship.
  • 稗官 - Minor officials or low-ranking bureaucrats.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 龙溪郑开僖识 - Lóngxī Zhèng Kāixī is a person's name, and '识' means 'to write' or 'to record,' indicating that this is the signature of the author.
  • 滦阳消夏录卷首语 - Title of the work, which translates to 'Preface to the Summer Record of Luanyang.'
  • 乾隆己酉夏 - Date in the traditional Chinese calendar, specifically the summer of the Ji (己) year of the You (酉) cycle during the Qianlong era (1789).
  • 以编排秘藉，于役滦阳 - '编排' means 'to compile and arrange,' '秘藉' refers to secret or rare books, and '于役' means 'to serve' or 'to be engaged in,' indicating that the author was working on compiling and arranging books in Luanyang.
  • 昼长无事，追录见闻，忆及即书，都无体例 - '昼长' means 'long days,' '无事' means 'nothing to do,' '追录' means 'to record,' '见闻' means 'what has been seen and heard,' '忆及即书' means 'as soon as remembered, it is written down,' and '都无体例' means 'without any fixed form or structure.' This phrase describes the author's idle time and his method of recording his thoughts and experiences.
  • 小说稗官，知无关于著述 - '小说' refers to short stories or novels, '稗官' means 'minor officials,' and '知无关于著述' means 'knowing they have no relation to serious writing.' This phrase suggests that the recorded stories are not considered serious literature.
  • 街谈巷议，或有益于劝惩 - '街谈巷议' means 'street talk and alley gossip,' and '或有益于劝惩' means 'may be beneficial for encouragement and punishment.' This phrase indicates that the stories might have moral or educational value.
  • 聊付抄胥存之，命曰《滦阳消夏录》云尔 - '聊付' means 'to casually entrust,' '抄胥' means 'copyist,' '存之' means 'to keep it,' '命曰' means 'to name it,' and '云尔' means 'so it is.' This phrase indicates that the author entrusted the records to a copyist to keep them, and he named the work 'Preface to the Summer Record of Luanyang.'
  • 卷一 滦阳消夏录「一」 - Title of the first volume, which translates to 'Volume One of the Summer Record of Luanyang.'
  • 神猪智狐谐鬼鬼诗梦中绝句精灵论诗无赖吕四人狐恋王半仙友狐郑苏仙梦冥府狂电穿人无云和尚宁波吴生老儒遇鬼河间唐生轻薄少年媚鬼逃遁贪吏遇鬼艳女说驿使人狐争居石壁出人语曹氏不怕鬼默庵先生戏术北窗怪声仙童诗有鬼魅梦中吟小花犬显灵神柏吕道士幻术马语侍姬烂舌某生刃妻画中景某医好毒六壬术士奇术僧胡维华慧女复仇鬼牒又一骆宾王老桑树凶宅沧州潘班夭逝女碣江宁书生 - A long list of titles or subjects, each representing a different story or anecdote. These titles are mostly self-explanatory, but some require brief explanations: '神猪智狐' - 'Divine Pig and Wise Fox', '谐鬼鬼诗' - 'Poetry of Harmonious Ghosts', '梦中绝句' - 'Absolute Sentences in Dreams', '精灵论诗' - 'Elves Discuss Poetry', '无赖吕四人' - 'Rascal Lu and Four Others', '狐恋王半仙' - 'Fox Loves Half-Saint Wang', '友狐郑苏仙' - 'Fox Friend Zheng and Su', '梦冥府' - 'Dream of the Underworld', '狂电穿人' - 'Mad Lightning Pierces People', '无云和尚' - 'Cloudless Monk', '宁波吴生' - 'Wu from Ningbo', '老儒遇鬼' - 'Old Scholar Meets a Ghost', '河间唐生' - 'Tang from Hejian', '轻薄少年媚鬼' - 'Flirtatious Youth Flirts with a Ghost', '逃遁贪吏' - 'Evading Greedy Officials', '遇鬼艳女' - 'Encounters with a Beautiful Ghost', '说驿使' - 'Messenger's Story', '人狐争居石壁' - 'Human and Fox Compete for a Stone Wall', '出人语' - 'Words Spoken by People', '曹氏不怕鬼' - 'Cao Family Not Afraid of Ghosts', '默庵先生戏术' - 'Mr. Mo'an's Tricks', '北窗怪声' - 'Strange Sounds from the North Window', '仙童诗' - 'Poetry of Immortal Children', '有鬼魅' - 'With Ghosts and Demons', '梦中吟' - 'Singing in Dreams', '小花犬显灵' - 'Little Flower Dog Manifests Spirit', '神柏' - 'Sacred Cypress', '吕道士幻术' - 'Lü Daoist's Illusions', '马语' - 'Horse Speaks', '侍姬烂舌' - 'Maid with a Loose Tongue', '某生刃妻' - 'A Student Slays His Wife', '画中景' - 'Scenes in Paintings', '某医好毒' - 'A Doctor Loves Poison', '六壬术士' - 'Six-Ren Diviner', '奇术' - 'Strange Arts', '僧胡维华' - 'Buddhist Monk Hu Weihua', '慧女复仇' - 'Wise Girl Takes Revenge', '鬼牒' - 'Ghost Records', '又一骆宾王' - 'Another Cao Binwang', '老桑树' - 'Old Mulberry Tree', '凶宅' - 'Haunted House', '沧州潘班' - 'Pan Ban from Cangzhou', '夭逝女碣' - 'Stele of a Young Woman Who Died Young', '江宁书生' - 'Scholar from Jiangning'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道光 - Reign title of Emperor Daoguang of the Qing dynasty (1820-1850).
  • 乾隆 - Reign title of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing dynasty (1735-1796).
  • 滦阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in Hebei Province.
  • 龙溪 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in Fujian Province.
  • 郑开僖 - The name of the author, likely a scholar or official of the Qing dynasty.
  • 神猪 - A divine pig, often associated with folklore and mythology.
  • 智狐 - A wise fox, often associated with trickery and intelligence in folklore.
  • 谐鬼 - Harmonious ghosts, often associated with playful or mischievous spirits.
  • 吕四人 - Four people named Lu, likely characters in the stories.
  • 王半仙 - A half-saint named Wang, often associated with supernatural abilities.
  • 郑苏仙 - A character named Zheng and Su, likely involved in a story.
  • 冥府 - The underworld, often associated with the afterlife and spirits.
  • 无云和尚 - A monk named Wuyun, likely a character in the stories.
  • 宁波 - A city in Zhejiang Province.
  • 河间 - A region in Hebei Province.
  • 骆宾王 - A famous poet of the Tang dynasty (618-907).
  • 沧州 - A city in Hebei Province.
  • 江宁 - A city in Jiangsu Province, now known as Nanjing.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷二 - Volume numbers, standard in classical Chinese texts, indicating the second volume of *Pulang Xiaxia Lu* (《滦阳消夏录》).
  • 卷三 - Volume numbers, standard in classical Chinese texts, indicating the third volume of *Pulang Xiaxia Lu* (《滦阳消夏录》).
  • 「二」 - Volume subheadings, commonly used to denote parts or sections within a volume.
  • 「三」 - Volume subheadings, commonly used to denote parts or sections within a volume.
  • 卜 - To divinate or predict the future, often using methods like yarrow stalks or oracle bones.
  • 扶乩 - A divination practice involving a spirit medium, often using a stick to write messages on sand or soil.
  • 問壽 - To ask about one's lifespan or longevity.
  • 舊玉馬 - A jade horse, likely referring to an ancient artifact or relic.
  • 狐女 - A female fox spirit, a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 情女 - A woman of romantic or emotional disposition, possibly a courtesan or romantic figure.
  • 酒鬼 - A drunkard or habitual drinker.
  • 產麟牛 - A mythical cow giving birth to a qilin (a Chinese unicorn-like creature), indicating an auspicious or miraculous event.
  • 空宅 - An empty house, often associated with haunting or supernatural activity.
  • 《埤雅》 - A classical Chinese encyclopedic text, *Biya* (《埤雅》), which discusses natural phenomena and includes folkloric material.
  • 綠笺 - Green paper or writing on green paper, often associated with poetry or love letters.
  • 夢中絕句 - A couplet or short poem composed in a dream.
  • 托夢 - To send a message through a dream.
  • 稱疾 - To claim illness, often as a pretext for avoiding duties or responsibilities.
  • 幻術 - Illusions or magic tricks, often associated with supernatural beings.
  • 人耶鬼耶 - Is it a human or a ghost? – a rhetorical question expressing uncertainty.
  • 大魚擊小奴 - A large fish strikes a small servant – likely a metaphor or allegory for power dynamics or supernatural events.
  • 神笞 - Divine punishment or beating, often a supernatural form of retribution.
  • 孝悌通神 - Filial piety and brotherly respect connect with the divine, a Confucian ideal.
  • 冥官 - An official of the underworld, a figure in Chinese cosmology.
  • 索藥 - To seek medicine, possibly from a supernatural source.
  • 永公 - A title or name, possibly a monk or religious figure, though the exact identity is unclear.
  • 游僧 - A wandering monk, often associated with supernatural or mystical practices.
  • 托夢稱疾 - To send a dream and claim illness – a combination of supernatural and practical actions.
  • 桐園女子 - A woman from the桐园 (Tongyuan), likely a specific location or a symbolic setting.
  • 判官 - An official in the underworld, often associated with the administration of the afterlife.
  • 冥吏 - A clerk or official in the underworld.
  • 姚安公之仆 - A servant of Lord Yao'an, a specific person or figure in the narrative.
  • 廢祠 - A ruined or abandoned shrine, often associated with lingering spirits or supernatural activity.
  • 里胥 - A village official or local magistrate, responsible for minor legal and administrative duties.
  • 鞭囚 - To whip a prisoner, indicating punishment or justice.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《埤雅》 - A classical Chinese encyclopedic text written by Zhu Quan (朱雀), a scholar of the Northern Song dynasty. It discusses natural phenomena, animals, and includes folkloric and poetic elements.
  • 扶乩 - A form of divination practiced in Chinese folk religion, where a medium uses a stick to write messages from spirits.
  • 狐女 - Fox spirits are a recurring theme in Chinese folklore, often portrayed as beautiful, seductive, and supernatural beings.
  • 孝悌通神 - This phrase reflects the Confucian ideal that moral virtues like filial piety and respect for elders can connect one with the divine or supernatural.
  • 里胥 - A local official in the traditional Chinese administrative system, often involved in minor legal matters and tax collection.
  • 姚安公 - A title or name of a noble or official, possibly a real historical figure or a fictional character in the text.
  • 永公 - A name or title, possibly a monk or religious figure, but the exact identity is unclear.
  • 桐園女子 - A woman from Tongyuan, which may be a real location or a symbolic setting in the narrative.
  • 游僧 - Wandering monks were common figures in Chinese literature and religious texts, often associated with supernatural or mystical experiences.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戈壁蝎虎林 - Compound term referring to a desert or arid region with specific characteristics, possibly metaphorical or descriptive of a particular landscape.
  • 黑气 - Can mean 'dark vapor' or 'dark energy,' often used in mystical or supernatural contexts.
  • 神马槐家镇 - Proper name, likely a place name, but the exact meaning and significance are unclear without further context.
  • 前明之僧 - Refers to a monk from the Ming dynasty, indicating a historical or cultural reference.
  • 狐居小楼 - Describes a fox living in a small building, often used in folklore or supernatural stories.
  • 钱宅雅狐 - Refers to a refined fox in a wealthy family's residence, suggesting a story involving a fox spirit.
  • 河间冯树痴婢辩火 - Complex phrase that likely refers to a specific story or event involving a woman named Feng Shuchih and a dispute over fire.
  • 李某诱妇荔姐遇鬼 - Describes a man named Li who lured a woman named Lijie to encounter a ghost.
  • 有鬼雪仇 - Means 'a ghost avenges a grudge,' indicating a supernatural revenge plot.
  • 破寺蠢僧 - Refers to a foolish monk in a ruined temple, often used in comedic or satirical contexts.
  • 娈童姊妹狐 - Describes a pair of fox spirits who are sisters and may have a relationship with young boys.
  • 乌鲁木齐巴拉红丝绣花鞋 - Refers to a specific item or event, possibly a story involving a woman named Balahong and her red silk embroidered shoes.
  • 郭六有憾于君 - Indicates a person named Guo Liu has a grudge against someone.
  • 宋小岩温雅小童 - Describes a gentle and refined young boy named Song Xiaoyan.
  • 烈女有灵 - Refers to a virtuous woman who has supernatural powers or a spirit.
  • 刘羽冲魏忠贤之恶 - Involves a person named Liu Yuchong and the evil deeds of Wei Zhongxian, a notorious eunuch during the Ming dynasty.
  • 红柳娃塞外雪莲 - Describes a story involving a girl named Hong Liuwa and a snow lotus in the border region.
  • 风灾鬼难之域 - Refers to a place where natural disasters and supernatural troubles occur.
  • 明季书生 - Refers to a scholar from the late Ming dynasty.
  • 卧虎山人孟夫人 - Describes a woman named Meng Furen who is associated with a recluse named Wuhu Shanren.
  • 魏藻枯井 - Involves a person named Wei Zao and a dry well.
  • 卫瞽巨盗 - Refers to a blind man named Wei who is a large-scale thief.
  • 齐大梁上狐 - Describes a fox on the wall of a building named Qida Liang.
  • 语甲乙丙 - A common phrase in classical Chinese, often used to list items or people in a sequence.
  • 秋谷先生 - Refers to a specific person, possibly a scholar or official.
  • 道士卖药 - Describes a Taoist priest selling medicine.
  • 生死有命 - A common phrase meaning 'life and death are determined by fate.'
  • 冷掌如冰 - Describes a cold hand like ice, often used to describe a person's demeanor or physical state.
  • 鬼趣 - Refers to the nature or behavior of ghosts.
  • 王半仙访狐友 - Describes a half-adept named Wang visiting a fox friend.
  • 鬼气吏为狐媚 - Refers to a ghostly official being charmed by a fox.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 前明之僧 - Refers to the Ming dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
  • 魏忠贤之恶 - Wei Zhongxian was a notorious eunuch who held significant power during the late Ming dynasty and was known for his corrupt and oppressive actions.
  • 明季书生 - The late Ming dynasty was a period of political turmoil and social change, often depicted in literature and folklore.
  • 魏藻枯井 - The name 'Wei Zao' and the context of a dry well suggest a specific story or event, possibly involving a moral or supernatural element.
  • 齐大梁上狐 - The name 'Qida Liang' and the context of a fox on a wall suggest a specific location or story, possibly involving a supernatural element.
  • 秋谷先生 - The name 'Qiu Gu' suggests a specific person, possibly a scholar or official, whose life or works are significant in the context of the story.
  • 道士卖药 - Taoist priests selling medicine were common in traditional Chinese society, often associated with healing and spiritual practices.
  • 生死有命 - This phrase reflects the traditional Chinese belief in fate and destiny, often used in philosophical and literary contexts.
  • 冷掌如冰 - This phrase is often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a person's demeanor or physical state, particularly in supernatural or ghost stories.
  • 鬼趣 - The concept of 'ghost nature' or 'ghost behavior' is a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature, often used to explore the supernatural and the unknown.
  • 王半仙访狐友 - The term 'half-adept' (半仙) refers to a person who has achieved some level of spiritual or magical power but is not fully enlightened. The story of Wang visiting a fox friend likely involves elements of supernatural interaction.
  • 鬼气吏为狐媚 - This phrase suggests a story involving a ghostly official being charmed by a fox, which is a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature, often used to explore the intersection of the supernatural and human affairs.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷六 - Standard division in classical Chinese texts; 'Volume 6'.
  • 滦阳消夏录 - Title of a literary collection; possibly a collection of strange tales or anecdotes. 'Luanyang' may refer to a place, and 'Xiaxia Lu' could be 'Summer Notes' or 'Summer Record'.
  • 「六」 - Possibly a subtitle or reference to the sixth chapter or section within the book.
  • 立墓 - Literally 'to build a tomb'; in context, may refer to a ghost or spirit that has built a tomb for itself or someone else.
  • 天竺老僧 - 'Tianzhu' refers to ancient India; '老僧' is 'old monk'; may refer to a legendary Indian monk.
  • 林鬼役鬼 - '林鬼' may mean 'forest spirits' or 'ghosts of the woods'; '役鬼' means 'to command spirits'; could refer to a ghost or spirit that controls other ghosts.
  • 符咒 - Refers to talismans or magical inscriptions used in Daoist or folk magic.
  • 二鬼城隍 - '城隍' is the deity of a city or town; '二鬼' could mean 'two ghosts' or 'two spirit entities' associated with the city god.
  • 破棺 - 'To break open a coffin'; possibly referring to a ghost or spirit emerging from a coffin.
  • 粤东异僧 - '粤东' refers to the eastern part of Guangdong province; '异僧' means 'strange monk' or 'unusual monk'; possibly a supernatural or eccentric figure.
  • 江南崔寅壁中人面鬼 - '江南' is 'south of the river' or 'southern Jiangnan'; '崔寅' is a name; '壁中人面鬼' could be 'a ghost with a human face in the wall' or 'a ghost hidden behind a wall'.
  • 隐乔车 - '隐乔' may be a name or term; '车' is 'carriage' or 'vehicle'; might refer to a ghost or spirit associated with a hidden carriage or vehicle.
  • 二幕友 - '幕友' refers to a retainer or assistant; '二' means 'two'; could be 'two assistants' or 'two literary associates'.
  • 狐之报 - '狐' is 'fox'; '报' is 'retribution' or 'reward'; likely refers to a fox spirit exacting vengeance or karma.
  • 取子偿冤 - '取子' may mean 'to take a child' or 'to take the child as payment'; '偿冤' is 'to repay a grudge' or 'to settle a grievance'; could refer to a ghost or spirit taking a child as retribution for a past wrong.
  • 侠牛瑞草天生梵字大悲咒 - '侠牛' may be a name or term; '瑞草' is 'auspicious grass' or 'miraculous plant'; '天生' is 'born naturally'; '梵字' refers to Sanskrit characters or sacred script; '大悲咒' is the 'Great Compassion Mantra' from Buddhist tradition.
  • 喇嘛黑影扑人 - '喇嘛' is a Tibetan Buddhist monk or lama; '黑影' is 'black shadow' or 'dark figure'; '扑人' is 'to pounce on a person'; could describe a ghost or supernatural being in the form of a lama.
  • 燕园公张说秃项马真缢鬼 - '燕园' may refer to a garden or estate; '公' is 'lord' or 'official'; '张说' is a name; '秃项' is 'bald neck' or 'bald head'; '马真' is another name; '缢鬼' is 'hanged ghost' or 'ghost of someone who was strangled'.
  • 善恶之报 - Common phrase meaning 'the retribution of good and evil'; a concept from Buddhist and Daoist teachings.
  • 刘乙斋笔棒楼徐景熹青苗神陈太夫人道士神符蜃气城影梦神诃责陈生张巡妾转世索命风怪南村董天士快哉行堕马者巨盗齐舜庭娈童惨绿袍夜读少年八蜡祠道士乱山归途好名之鬼黑驴精河间妇不寐术梦冥司吏梦神示诗龟卜魂飞冥府饿鬼小儿见奇鬼 - Long list of titles, names, and terms from the *Luanyang Xiaxia Lu* collection; includes names like *刘乙斋* (Liu Yizhai), *徐景熹* (Xu Jingxi), *青苗神* (Green Sprout God), *陈太夫人* (Madam Chen), *道士神符* (Taoist divine talisman), *蜃气* (mirage), *城影* (city shadow), *梦神* (dream god), *诃责陈生* (rebuke Chen Student), *张巡妾* (concubine of Zhang Xun), *转世索命* (reincarnated to claim a life), *风怪* (wind demon), *南村董天士* (Dong Tianshi of Nancun), *快哉行* (poetic title), *堕马者* (the one who fell from a horse), *巨盗齐舜庭* (great thief Qi Shunting), *娈童惨绿袍* (lover boy in green robe), *夜读少年* (youth reading at night), *八蜡祠* (Eight Candles Shrine), *道士乱山归途* (Taoist in the mountains on the way home), *好名之鬼* (ghost obsessed with fame), *黑驴精* (black donkey spirit), *河间妇不寐术梦* (woman from Hejian who does not sleep and has magical dreams), *冥司吏* (official of the underworld), *梦神示诗* (dream god shows a poem), *龟卜魂飞冥府* (turtle divination, soul flies to the underworld), *饿鬼小儿见奇鬼* (hunger ghost child sees a strange ghost).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 滦阳消夏录 - Title of a classical Chinese literary collection; *Luanyang* may be a misspelling or variant of *Luanyang*, a city in modern-day Hebei Province; *Xiaxia Lu* can be translated as 'Summer Notes' or 'Notes of the Summer Season', suggesting a collection of stories or anecdotes recorded during the summer months.
  • 天竺老僧 - 'Tianzhu' refers to ancient India; '老僧' is 'old monk'; may refer to a legendary Indian monk.
  • 城隍 - Local deity in Chinese folk religion; believed to protect a city or town; *城隍庙* is the temple dedicated to this deity.
  • 狐之报 - 'Huxi' (fox spirits) are common in Chinese folklore; often represent both tricksters and supernatural beings with human-like qualities; '报' (retribution) suggests a narrative of karmic justice.
  • 大悲咒 - 'Dabai Zuo' is the 'Great Compassion Mantra' from Buddhist tradition; often recited for protection and mercy.
  • 喇嘛 - 'Lama' refers to a Tibetan Buddhist monk or lama.

  • 滦阳 - Possibly a misspelling or variant of *Luanyang*, a city in modern-day Hebei Province.
  • 天竺 - Classical Chinese term for ancient India.
  • 城隍 - Local deity in Chinese folk religion; believed to protect a city or town.
  • 张巡 - Historical figure; *妾* refers to his concubine.
  • 刘乙斋 - Name, possibly of an author or character.
  • 徐景熹 - Name.
  • 陈太夫人 - Madam Chen; possibly a noblewoman or figure.
  • 齐舜庭 - Name; '巨盗' refers to a great thief.
  • 董天士 - Name; possibly of a character.
  • 八蜡祠 - Possibly a religious or historical site; 'Eight Candles Shrine' or 'Eight Sacrificial Altars'.
  • 河间妇 - Woman from Hejian.
  • 冥司吏 - Official of the underworld.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如是我聞 - Standard Buddhist opening phrase meaning 'Thus have I heard', used to introduce a sutra or teaching.
  • 柿園敗將 - Metaphorical phrase referring to a defeated general or someone who has failed in their endeavors.
  • 烈婦英靈 - Refers to the spirit of a heroic woman who has died in a noble cause.
  • 旱魃為虐 - Describes a severe drought caused by a malevolent spirit.
  • 奇井柩中巨禽魂錄冥籍 - Describes an unusual event where a large bird is found in a coffin in a strange well, and its soul is recorded in the records of the underworld.
  • 里人阎勋梦神高论 - Describes a villager named Yan Xun who dreams of a deity delivering profound teachings.
  • 夜见二叟 - Describes a nocturnal encounter with two elderly men.
  • 鬼方灵验 - Refers to a place or region known for its spiritual efficacy.
  • 二鬼求食 - Describes two spirits seeking sustenance.
  • 狐窟教子 - Refers to a fox den where a fox teaches its offspring.
  • 恶作剧拆字术 - Describes a prank involving the manipulation of Chinese characters.
  • 医者胡宫山蓬首垢面 - Describes a physician with unkempt hair and dirty face.
  • 人缢鬼变形求代 - Describes a person who commits suicide and a ghost that transforms to take their place.
  • 刘鬼谷事奇理真 - Refers to a story about Liu Guiguzi, a legendary figure known for his wisdom and supernatural abilities.
  • 枭鸟与破镜 - Refers to a story involving an owl and a broken mirror.
  • 老魅吟歌 - Describes an old witch singing.
  • 空中人语 - Describes voices heard in the air.
  • 张氏兄弟千古痴魂 - Refers to the souls of the Zhang brothers who have been in a state of eternal love.
  • 尤物复仇自缢贞女怨鬼投诉观心 - Describes a beautiful woman who commits suicide in revenge and a virtuous woman who becomes a ghost to complain about injustice.
  • 盗句狐妖报德怨仙 - Describes a fox spirit who steals a poem and a resentful deity.
  • 杏病牛思主不祥之物 - Describes a sick tree and a cow thinking of its master, and something ominous.
  • 鬼者说鬼 - Describes a story told by a ghost.
  • 地下眷属 - Refers to the deceased's family in the afterlife.
  • 青面罗刹 - Refers to a blue-faced demon.
  • 鬼狐戏人 - Describes ghosts and fox spirits playing tricks on people.
  • 狂士沧州太学生 - Refers to a mad scholar who is a student at the Cangzhou Academy.
  • 龙凤地群狐悬人 - Describes a place where foxes hang people.
  • 淫狐山神狐化幼妇选人遇狐神兔卖花者 - Describes a story involving a promiscuous fox, a mountain god, a fox that transforms into a young woman, a person who meets a fox spirit, and a rabbit who sells flowers.
  • 黑狐某公多疑二鬼争墓狐具人心丐者宦家子佣媪语冥司 - Describes a black fox, a suspicious official, two ghosts fighting over a grave, a fox with a human heart, a beggar, a nobleman's son, a servant woman, and a conversation with the underworld officials.
  • 可惜 - Common expression meaning 'too bad' or 'a pity'.

  • 善博者 - A skilled gambler, a character trait or role in the story.
  • 與狐女生子者 - One who has a child with a fox woman, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 廢宅 - Abandoned house, often a setting for supernatural stories.
  • 虎神磷為鬼火 - The tiger spirit’s phosphorescence becomes ghost fire, a poetic or metaphorical description.
  • 二僧之敗 - The downfall of two monks, likely referring to a story or anecdote.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 如是我聞 - Direct borrowing from Buddhist *Sutra* titles, suggesting inspiration from Buddhist or Daoist traditions.
  • 邱长春 - Refers to the historical Daoist figure *Qiu Changchun* (丘處機), though with the prefix *假* (false).
  • 冥官 - Officials of the underworld, part of the bureaucratic system of the afterlife in Chinese traditions.
  • 無常者 - The *Wuchang* (無常), messengers of death in Chinese folklore.
  • 冥事 - Matters of the afterlife, including judgment and supernatural affairs.
  • 夙緣 - Karmic or fated relationships, a concept from Buddhism and Taoism.
  • 圓光術 - Divination or spiritual vision, possibly involving a circle of light.
  • 書生 - A scholar or literatus, often a protagonist in Chinese supernatural stories.
  • 狐媚 - Fox seduction, a common motif involving fox spirits (huli jing).
  • 鬼魅 - Ghosts or spirits, often portrayed as vengeful or cursed beings.
  • 報恩 - Repaying a debt of gratitude, a moral theme in Chinese stories.
  • 善惡有報 - Moral maxim meaning 'good and evil are rewarded accordingly.'
  • 人狐夙緣 - Predestined relationships between humans and fox spirits.
  • 冥司之法 - Laws of the underworld, reflecting a structured afterlife.

Proper Nouns

  • 邱长春 - Reference to the historical Daoist figure *Qiu Changchun* (丘處機), with *假* (false) indicating an imposter version.
  • 青縣張公 - Refers to a historical or fictional official from Qing County (青縣) named Zhang.
  • 姚安公 - Title or name of a person, possibly a scholar or official.
  • 束州邵氏子 - Reference to a person from Shuzhou (束州) named Shao.
  • 李氏兄弟 - The Li brothers, likely characters from the story.
  • 泰州任子 - A person from Taizhou (泰州) named Ren.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如是我聞「四」 - Title, likely meaning 'Thus I have heard (Four)' or 'As I heard (Four)', suggesting this is the fourth in a series of texts titled 如是我聞 (Thus I have heard), which may be a collection of stories or moral teachings.
  • 冥司 - Literally 'underworld office' or 'underworld administration,' referring to the bureaucratic system of the afterlife in Chinese cosmology.
  • 狐據書樓 - Fox occupies a book pavilion – a metaphor or literal description of a fox (often used in Chinese folklore to represent a shapeshifting spirit or demon) taking possession of a place of learning or knowledge.
  • 冥女還魂 - Underworld woman returns to life – refers to a female spirit from the afterlife returning to the physical world, possibly through reincarnation or possession.
  • 臨陣憶斯語 - Recall these words in battle – suggests a moment of recollection or realization in the midst of a critical situation, such as a military conflict.
  • 奢儉有度 - Luxury and frugality have their limits – a balanced view of spending and restraint.
  • 戒殺牛 - Advise against killing cattle – a moral or religious injunction against the slaughter of animals, possibly linked to Buddhist or Daoist teachings.
  • 重規疊矩 - Repeating the same rules – a phrase meaning to follow established customs or standards.
  • 寡婦盡孝 - A widow fulfills filial piety – a moral example of a woman showing devotion to her family, especially after the death of her husband.
  • 巫能視鬼 - The witch can see ghosts – a reference to the supernatural abilities of a shaman or sorcerer.
  • 《佐治藥言》 - A title of a classical Chinese text, possibly a treatise or compilation of advice on governance and moral conduct. The term 藥言 (medicinal words) suggests that the text contains moral or ethical teachings meant to 'cure' societal ills.
  • 子不語怪 - The Master does not speak of strange things – a reference to Confucius' teachings, which emphasize the importance of focusing on moral and social duties rather than supernatural phenomena.
  • 孝廉 - A title in the Han Dynasty for a person recommended for official service based on moral character and filial piety.
  • 宦家義婦 - A virtuous woman from a bureaucratic family – a woman of high social standing who demonstrates moral integrity.
  • 野狐戲人 - A wild fox plays with people – a metaphor for a fox spirit or demon deceiving or tricking humans, often used in folktales.
  • 人偽狐狀 - A human imitating a fox’s form – a reference to a human pretending to be a fox or a fox spirit taking human form.
  • 放生 - Releasing living beings – a Buddhist practice of freeing animals from captivity, considered a meritorious act.
  • 董鄂 - A name, possibly referring to a historical or literary figure, or a place. 董鄂 is also a surname, and there is a famous historical figure, 董鄂妃 (Dong’e Fei), the consort of Emperor Shunzhi of the Qing Dynasty.
  • 張君平 - A name, possibly referring to a historical figure or a literary character. 張君平 may be a reference to Zhang Junping, a figure from the Tang Dynasty, or a fictional character.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冥司 - The 冥司 (Underworld Administration) is a concept from Chinese religious and folk beliefs, often depicted as a bureaucratic system in the afterlife, similar to the Western concept of Hades. It includes judges, record-keepers, and officials who oversee the souls of the deceased.
  • 狐 - The fox (hú) is a recurring symbol in Chinese folklore, often representing cunning, transformation, and supernatural power. In stories, foxes are frequently depicted as hu (fox spirits) who can transform into humans and interact with the human world.
  • 冥女 - A 冥女 is a woman from the afterlife, possibly a ghost or spirit who has returned to the living world. In some stories, these spirits may be seeking redemption, revenge, or a second chance at life.
  • 《佐治藥言》 - This title suggests a text that offers practical advice for governance, possibly in the form of moral instruction or policy recommendations. The term 藥言 (medicinal words) implies that the text is meant to 'cure' or improve society.
  • 子不語怪 - This is a reference to Analects of Confucius (《論語》), where Confucius is said to not speak of strange or supernatural matters. The phrase is used to emphasize the importance of focusing on moral and ethical conduct rather than the supernatural.
  • 孝廉 - This was a system in the Han Dynasty where individuals were recommended for government office based on their moral character and filial piety. It was a form of merit-based selection.
  • 宦家 - A term for families of officials or bureaucrats, indicating a high social status in traditional Chinese society.
  • 義婦 - A term for a woman who demonstrates extraordinary moral virtue, often in the face of adversity or tragedy.
  • 放生 - This is a common Buddhist practice, especially in East Asia, where devotees release animals from captivity as an act of compassion and merit-making.
  • 董鄂 - As mentioned earlier, this could be a reference to 董鄂妃 (Dong’e Fei), the consort of Emperor Shunzhi of the Qing Dynasty, who was highly revered and died young. Alternatively, it may be a fictional or literary name.
  • 張君平 - If this refers to Zhang Junping, it may be a reference to a Tang Dynasty figure or a literary character. Further context is needed to determine the exact identity.

Proper Nouns

  • 如是我聞 - A title, possibly modeled after the Buddhist 如是我聞 (Thus I have heard), which is a standard opening phrase in Buddhist texts.
  • 《佐治藥言》 - A proper title of a classical text, possibly a treatise on governance and morality.
  • 董鄂 - A name, possibly referring to a historical figure or a literary character.
  • 張君平 - A name, possibly referring to a historical or literary figure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 文以载道 - Classical phrase meaning 'writing conveys the Way,' emphasizing the moral and philosophical purpose of literature.
  • 儒者无不能言之 - Means 'scholars cannot fail to speak of it,' indicating that the concept of '道' (the Way) is universally acknowledged among scholars.
  • 道岂深隐莫测秘密不传 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is the Way so deep and hidden, secret and untransmittable?' This phrase challenges the notion that '道' is obscure or esoteric.
  • 万事当然之理，是即道矣 - Means 'the natural principles of all things are the Way,' equating the Way with universal truths.
  • 文之大者为六经 - Means 'the greatest of writings are the Six Classics,' referring to the foundational texts of Confucianism.
  • 降而为列朝之史，降而为诸子之书，降而为百氏之集 - Series of descending statements indicating that the Six Classics are followed by historical records, works of the various schools of thought, and collections of writings by many authors.
  • 再降而稗官小说，似无与于道矣 - Means 'even further down to the writings of minor officials and novels, it seems to have nothing to do with the Way,' suggesting that these forms are less significant.
  • 然汉书艺文志列为一家 - Means 'yet the *Han Shu* (Book of Han) *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) lists them as a category,' indicating that these works still have a place in the literary canon.
  • 历代书目亦皆著录 - Means 'the bibliographies of all dynasties also record them,' emphasizing their inclusion in historical records.
  • 岂非以荒诞悖妄者 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is it not because of the absurd and heretical ones?' This suggests that the inclusion of these works is due to their controversial nature.
  • 其近于正者，于人心世道亦未尝无所裨欤 - Means 'those that are close to the right, have not been without benefit to the hearts of people and the ways of the world,' indicating that even seemingly frivolous works can have some positive influence.
  • 河间先生以学问文章负天下重望 - Means 'Mr. Hejian, with his learning and literary talent, bears the great reputation of the world,' praising the scholar's reputation.
  • 天性孤直 - Means 'by nature solitary and upright,' describing the scholar's character.
  • 不喜以心性空谈标榜门户 - Means 'he does not like to engage in empty talk about the mind and nature to establish a school,' indicating a dislike for philosophical debates.
  • 亦不喜才人放诞诗社酒社，夸名士风流 - Means 'he also does not like talented people who are unrestrained in poetry societies and drinking clubs, flaunting the elegance of the literati,' showing a disapproval of certain literary and social behaviors.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六经 - The Six Classics, the foundational texts of Confucianism: *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
  • 汉书艺文志 - The *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) in the *Han Shu* (Book of Han), a historical text that categorizes various forms of literature.
  • 河间先生 - Refers to Mr. Hejian, likely a scholar or intellectual figure.

Proper Nouns

  • 文以载道 - A classical phrase emphasizing the moral and philosophical purpose of literature.
  • 儒者 - Confucian scholars.
  • 道 - A central concept in Chinese philosophy.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics of Confucianism.
  • 列朝之史 - Historical records of various dynasties.
  • 诸子之书 - Works of the various schools of thought.
  • 百氏之集 - Collections of writings by many authors.
  • 稗官小说 - Writings of minor officials and novels.
  • 汉书艺文志 - The *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) in the *Han Shu* (Book of Han).
  • 历代书目 - Bibliographies of all dynasties.
  • 河间先生 - Mr. Hejian, a scholar or intellectual figure.
  • 河间 - A place name, possibly referring to a region or a specific location associated with the scholar.

[14]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是以退食之余 - Classical expression indicating transition from duty to leisure; 'Therefore, after retiring from official duties.'
  • 惟耽怀典籍 - 'Only indulged in classical texts.' '耽怀' means to indulge or be deeply immersed in something.
  • 老而懒于考索 - 'Old age made him lazy in searching and investigating.' '考索' means to investigate or research.
  • 采掇异闻 - 'Collected strange or unusual stories.' '采掇' is to gather or collect.
  • 时作笔记 - 'Occasionally wrote notes.' '时作' means 'occasionally' or 'from time to time.'
  • 以寄所欲言 - 'To express what he wished to say.' Metaphorical way of saying notes served as a means of expressing thoughts.
  • 俶诡奇谲无所不载 - 'Strange and bizarre things, nothing is not included.' '俶诡奇谲' is a compound term describing strange and unusual things.
  • 洸洋恣肆无所不言 - 'Free-flowing and unrestrained, nothing is not spoken of.' '洸洋' and '恣肆' suggest a free and unrestrained style of writing.
  • 大旨要归于醇正 - 'The main intention ultimately returns to sincerity and correctness.' '醇正' means sincere, pure, or orthodox.
  • 欲使人知所劝惩 - 'To make people understand what to be encouraged or discouraged from.' '劝惩' is a classical term for moral instruction.
  • 诲淫导欲之书 - 'Books that teach immorality or guide toward lust.' Refers to books promoting immoral or sensual themes.
  • 以佳人才子相矜者 - 'Those that pride themselves on beautiful women and talented scholars.' Classical literary trope referring to romantic or sentimental stories.
  • 虽纸贵一时 - 'Though once as valuable as paper that was in great demand.' '纸贵' is an idiom meaning a work is very popular and widely circulated.
  • 终渐归湮没 - 'Eventually gradually became forgotten.' '湮没' means to be buried or forgotten.
  • 梨枣屡镌 - 'Repeatedly engraved on wooden blocks.' '梨枣' refers to the wooden blocks used in printing.
  • 久而不厌 - 'Long-lasting and not tiring.' Refers to the enduring popularity of the work.
  • 华实不同之明验矣 - 'A clear proof of the difference between appearance and reality.' '华实' is a metaphor for appearance (华) versus substance (实).
  • 顾翻刻者众，讹误实繁 - 'However, many reprints have been made, and errors are numerous.' '讹误' means errors or mistakes.
  • 妄为标目 - 'Arbitrarily adding titles.' '标目' refers to headings or titles.
  • 如明人之刻冷斋夜话者 - 'Like the Ming dynasty editions of *Lengzai Yewa*.' Refers to a specific classical text, *Lengzai Yewa* (Cold斋 Night Talk).
  • 读者病焉 - 'This troubles the readers.' '病' here is used metaphorically to mean 'bothers' or 'causes inconvenience.'
  • 时彦夙从先生游 - 'Shiyan has long been a student of the Master.' '夙从' means 'has long followed' or 'has long been a student of.'
  • 尝刻先生姑妄听之 - 'Once printed *Guwang Tingzhi* (Casually Listening) of the Master.' Refers to one of the five books, *Guwang Tingzhi*.
  • 附跋书尾 - 'Attached a preface at the end of the book.' '跋' is a preface or colophon.
  • 先生颇以为知言 - 'The Master considered this a wise comment.' '知言' means 'understanding words' or 'wise commentary.'
  • 迩来诸板益漫漶 - 'Recently, the various editions have become more blurred or worn out.' '漫漶' refers to worn, blurred, or illegible text.
  • 请于先生，合五书为一编 - 'He requested permission from the Master to compile the five books into one volume.' '一编' is a single volume or compilation.
  • 仍各存其原第 - 'While still preserving the original order of each book.' '原第' means the original sequence or order.
  • 篝灯手校不敢惮劳 - 'He painstakingly proofread by lamp, without fearing labor.' '篝灯' is a lamp made from a bamboo basket, used for reading or writing.
  • 又请先生检视一过 - 'Then he asked the Master to review it once more.' '检视' means to inspect or check.
  • 然后摹印 - 'Then he made a print.' '摹印' refers to the process of engraving or printing.
  • 虽先生之著作不必藉此刻以传 - 'Though the Master's writings do not necessarily need this edition to be preserved.' '藉' means to rely on or depend on.
  • 鱼鲁之舛差 - 'Differences in characters such as 鱼 and 鲁.' Refers to textual errors or variations in characters, such as the famous '鱼鲁' error in the *Book of Songs*.
  • 稀于先生教世之本志 - 'Are less important than the Master’s fundamental purpose of teaching the world.' '教世' is the idea of teaching or guiding the world, '本志' is the fundamental intention.
  • 或亦不无小补云尔 - 'Perhaps it is not without some small benefit.' A polite way of concluding that the effort, while not essential, is still of some use.
  • 嘉庆庚申八月门人北平盛时彦谨序 - 'In the eighth month of the year Gengshen of the Jiaqing era, a student from Beiping, Sheng Shiyan, respectfully wrote the preface.' This is the colophon or preface of the text.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 滦阳消夏录等五书 - Refers to five works by Pu Songling, the author of *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio). These five works are *Luan Yang Xia Xia Lu* (Notes on Summer in Luanyang), *Guanwang Tingzhi* (Casually Listening), *Liaozhai Zhiyi*, *Liaozhai Yishi* (Strange Stories from Liaozhai, Another Edition), and *Liaozhai Yizhi* (Strange Stories from Liaozhai, Another Edition).
  • 冷斋夜话 - *Lengzai Yewa* (Cold斋 Night Talk), a classical collection of anecdotes and literary discussions by the Song dynasty poet and scholar, Jiaoran (Jia Dao).
  • 鱼鲁之舛差 - Refers to the famous textual error in the *Book of Songs* where the character 鱼 (fish) was mistakenly written as 鲁 (Lu, a state), highlighting the importance of accurate textual transmission.
  • 嘉庆庚申 - The year 1810, the Gengshen year of the Jiaqing reign of the Qing dynasty.
  • 北平 - A former name for Beijing, used in the Qing dynasty.
  • 盛时彦 - The author of the preface, a student of Pu Songling. He is a historical figure known for compiling and editing Pu’s works.
  • 门人 - A student or disciple, used in classical Chinese to refer to a follower or apprentice.
  • 谨序 - A respectful preface or colophon, common in classical Chinese texts.

  • 不知李林甫、秦桧即不倾陷善类，亦作宰相，徒自增罪案耳 - A complex sentence that means 'even if Li Linfu and Qin Hui did not betray virtuous people, they still became prime ministers, but it would only add to their own crimes.' This highlights the futility of seeking power without moral integrity.
  • 天地之生才，朝廷之设官，所以补救气数也 - Means 'the creation of talents by heaven and earth, and the establishment of officials by the court, are all for the purpose of correcting the course of destiny.' This emphasizes the role of individuals in shaping their fate.
  • 束手而委命 - Literally means 'to fold one's hands and entrust one's fate,' indicating passivity and resignation.

  • 鞠躬尽瘁，死而后已 - A well-known phrase from Zhuge Liang, meaning 'to serve with utmost dedication until death,' emphasizing unwavering commitment.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 德州田白岩曰 - Standard narrative opening meaning 'Tian Baiyan of Dezhou said,' introducing the speaker and location.
  • 在滇黔間山行 - Phrase meaning 'traveling through the mountains between Yunnan and Guizhou,' referring to the region in southwest China.
  • 見道士按一麗女於石 - Saw a Daoist pressing a beautiful woman against a stone. '按' here may imply restraining or holding down.
  • 欲剖其心 - Intended to cut out her heart. A metaphor for extracting the soul or essence.
  • 女噭然一声，化火光飞去 - The woman let out a scream and transformed into a flame of fire, flying away. '噭然' is an onomatopoeic expression for a sharp, sudden sound.
  • 公败吾事 - You have ruined my matter or foiled my plan. A colloquial expression.
  • 此魅已媚杀百余人 - This demon has seduced and killed over a hundred people. '媚杀' is a compound verb meaning 'to seduce and kill.'
  • 取精已多，岁久通灵 - It has taken in much essence over time, and after many years, it has become spiritual. '取精' refers to the act of absorbing spiritual energy or essence.
  • 斩其首则神遁去 - If you behead it, its spirit will flee. A belief in Daoist or folk magic that killing the head does not necessarily destroy the spirit.
  • 必剖其心乃死 - Only by cutting out its heart will it die. A metaphor for destroying the core of a being.
  • 惜一猛虎之命，放置深山 - To spare the life of a tiger and release it into the deep mountains. A metaphor for letting a dangerous entity go.
  • 不知泽麋林鹿，劘其牙者几许命也 - Not knowing how many lives of deer and gazelle have been torn apart by its teeth. '劘其牙' means 'to gnaw with its teeth' or 'to tear with its fangs.'
  • 匣其匕首，恨恨渡溪去 - He placed his dagger into a box and angrily crossed the stream. '恨恨' expresses deep resentment or anger.
  • 即所谓一家哭何如一路哭也 - This is what is meant by 'What is the difference between one family weeping and an entire road weeping?' A reference to a classical Chinese idiom or saying.
  • 姑容墨吏，自以为阴功 - To tolerate corrupt officials, thinking it is a hidden virtue. '墨吏' refers to corrupt or unscrupulous officials.
  • 人亦多称为忠厚 - People also often call him a loyal and kind person. A common way of praising someone's character.
  • 穷民之卖儿贴妇，皆未一思 - The poor people who sell their sons and daughters, they have not even considered this once. A critique of the indifference of the powerful to the suffering of the poor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尊聖教也 - Classical explanatory clause ending, used to justify or explain the preceding action.
  • 昭帝王表章之功也 - Classical explanatory clause meaning 'to demonstrate the merit of the emperors in promoting and honoring the texts.'
  • 皆南面 - Classical expression meaning 'all face south,' indicating a formal arrangement, often used in Chinese court or temple settings to denote respect and hierarchy.
  • 每一部成，必取初印精好者 - Classical phrase meaning 'every time a new edition is completed, the first and finest print is selected.' This reflects a high standard of quality control.
  • 庋置斯閣 - Classical term meaning 'to store in this hall,' with '庋' being an archaic verb for storing or placing.
  • 獎汲古之勤也 - To reward the diligence in retrieving ancient texts. '汲古' is a classical term meaning 'to seek out ancient texts.'
  • 皆東西面 - 'All face east and west,' indicating that the private editions are placed in positions opposite to the central, official ones.
  • 後三楹則唐以前諸儒經義 - 'Behind the third column are the interpretations of the classics by scholars before the Tang dynasty.' '楹' is a classical term for a column or pillar.
  • 帙以纂組，收為一庫 - 'Bound in woven silk, collected into one vault.' '纂組' refers to a type of fine, woven silk used for binding or decoration.
  • 自是以外，雖著述等身，聲華蓋代，總聽其自貯名山，不得入此門一步焉 - 'Beyond this, even if one's writings fill the body and their fame surpasses the ages, they must store their works in their own sacred mountains and may not step into this gate even once.' This is a classical expression of exclusivity and reverence for the central collection.
  • 先聖之志也 - 'This is the will of the sage,' indicating that the arrangement is in accordance with the teachings of the sage.
  • 諸書至子刻午刻，一字一句，皆發濃香 - 'When the books reach the hour of Zi and Wu, each word and sentence emits a rich fragrance.' '子刻' and '午刻' are classical terms for the hours of Zi (11 PM to 1 AM) and Wu (11 AM to 1 PM), which were considered significant in the Chinese cosmological system.
  • 故題曰經香 - 'Hence it is called the 'Scent of the Classics' (經香),' indicating the symbolic or supernatural quality of the texts.
  • 蓋一元斡運，二氣絪缊 - 'Because the great cycle of the universe turns, and the two forces (yin and yang) intermingle.' This is a classical expression from Daoist cosmology.
  • 陰起午中，陽生子半 - 'Yin begins at the midpoint of Wu, and Yang is born at the midpoint of Zi.' This refers to the classical timing of the balance of yin and yang.
  • 聖人之心，與天地通 - 'The heart of the sage is in harmony with heaven and earth,' a classical expression of the sage’s unity with the cosmos.
  • 諸大儒闡發聖人之理，其精奧亦與天地通，故相感也 - 'The great scholars who explain the teachings of the sage, their profound insights also connect with heaven and earth, hence they are in resonance.' This is a metaphysical statement about the unity of human understanding and the cosmos.
  • 然必傳是學者始聞之，他人則否 - 'But only those who have passed on this learning can hear it; others cannot.' This implies that the knowledge is reserved for those who have properly studied and transmitted the teachings.

  • 此之谓矣 - Means 'this is what it refers to,' summarizing the analogy.

  • 皆根据古义 - Means 'all based on ancient meanings.'

  • 汉儒重师傅，渊源有自 - Means 'Han scholars valued teachers and had a clear lineage.'
  • 宋儒尚心悟，研索易深 - Means 'Song scholars valued introspection and delved deeply into their studies.'
  • 汉儒或执旧文，过于信传 - Means 'Han scholars sometimes adhered strictly to old texts and trusted them too much.'
  • 宋儒或凭臆断，勇于改经 - Means 'Song scholars sometimes relied on their own judgment and dared to alter the classics.'
  • 计其得失，亦复相当 - Means 'considering their gains and losses, they are roughly equal.'
  • 唯汉儒之学，非读书稽古，不能下一语 - Means 'only Han scholars' learning requires reading and studying ancient texts to make any statement.'

  • 此种虚构之词，亦非无因而作也 - Means 'such fabricated words are not made without reason.'

  • 或奇验，或全不验，或半验半不验 - A three-part list describing varying degrees of accuracy in predictions: sometimes astonishingly accurate, sometimes completely inaccurate, or sometimes partially accurate and partially inaccurate.
  • 余尝于闻见最确者，反复深思 - Means 'I have often pondered deeply on the most certain cases I have heard or seen,' indicating personal reflection on verified instances.
  • 八字贵贱贫富，特大略如是 - Means 'the eight characters (八字) indicating fortune, nobility, poverty, and wealth are only roughly like this,' referring to the traditional Chinese belief in fate based on birth dates.
  • 乘除盈缩 - Means 'addition and subtraction, surplus and deficit,' referring to the balancing or adjustment of fortunes.
  • 互相补矣 - Means 'they complement each other,' indicating mutual offsetting of advantages and disadvantages.
  • 寿考 - Means 'longevity,' referring to a long life.
  • 早寡 - Means 'widowed early,' referring to a woman who lost her husband at a young age.
  • 白首齐眉 - A metaphor meaning 'living together harmoniously until old age,' referring to a long and happy marriage.
  • 丧子 - Means 'losing a child,' referring to the death of a child.
  • 家计亦薄 - Means 'the household finances are also thin,' indicating financial hardship.
  • 盈虚消息 - Means 'fullness and emptiness, growth and decline,' referring to the natural cycle of fortunes.
  • 俟知命者更详之 - Means 'let those who understand fate elaborate further,' indicating a call for deeper analysis by experts.

  • 八字 - Refers to the eight characters in a person's birth date, used in Chinese astrology to predict fate.
  • 邹小山先生 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a scholar or official, with the courtesy name '小山.'

  • 礼部侍郎 - Refers to the Deputy Minister of Rites, a high-ranking official in the Chinese bureaucracy.
  • 盈虚消息 - Refers to the natural cycle of fortunes, a concept rooted in Chinese philosophy and astrology.

  • 知为神鬼 - Means 'he knew they were spirits or ghosts.'

  • 请颜先生一讲孝经可乎 - Means 'May Mr. Yan give a lecture on the *Xiaojing*?'
  • 颜回面向内曰 - Means 'Yan Hui, facing inward, said.'
  • 君小儿所诵，漏落颠倒，全非我所传本，我亦无可著语处 - Means 'What your children recited is missing and disordered, completely different from the version I taught, so I have nothing to say.'
  • 俄闻传王教曰 - Means 'Soon, he heard the king's command saying.'

  • 余谓此与爱堂先生所言学究遇冥吏事，皆博雅之士，造戏语以诟俗儒也 - Means 'I believe this is similar to what Mr. Aitang said about scholars encountering ghost officials, all of which are witty remarks by erudite scholars to mock common scholars.'

  • 人则真所杀 - Means 'the person was indeed killed by me,' a confession of murder.
  • 我即所杀之魂也 - Means 'I am the spirit of the person I killed,' identifying the speaker as the victim's spirit.

  • 家在海淀 - Means 'my home is in Haidian,' indicating the location of the spirit's residence.
  • 父曰李星望 - Means 'my father is Li Xingwang,' identifying the spirit's father.

  • 常明引我观灯归 - Means 'Changming took me to watch the lanterns and return home,' describing the event leading to the murder.
  • 夜深人寂 - Means 'deep into the night when everyone was quiet,' setting the time and atmosphere.
  • 常明戏调我 - Means 'Changming teased and flirted with me,' describing the perpetrator's actions.
  • 我方力拒 - Means 'I was trying to resist,' indicating the spirit's attempt to avoid the perpetrator.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先叔母高宜人之父，讳荣祉 - 先叔母 means 'my late aunt,' 高宜人 is a title for a woman of noble status, and 讳 is used to show respect when mentioning a deceased person's name. 荣祉 is the name of the deceased.
  • 官山西陵川令 - 官 means 'served as an official,' 山西 is a province in China, 陵川 is a county, and 令 means 'magistrate.'
  • 斫紫檀为座承之 - 斫 means 'to carve,' 紫檀 is a type of wood, 座 means 'base' or 'pedestal,' and 承之 means 'to support it.'
  • 恒置几上 - 恒 means 'always,' 置 means 'place,' and 几上 means 'on a table or desk.'
  • 前足本为双跪欲起之形 - 前足 means 'front legs,' 本为 means 'originally was,' 双跪 means 'kneeling on both knees,' 欲起 means 'about to rise,' and 之形 means 'shape.'
  • 一日左足忽伸出于座外 - 一日 means 'one day,' 左足 means 'left foot,' 忽 means 'suddenly,' 伸于 means 'extended out,' and 座外 means 'outside the base.'
  • 高公大骇，阁署传视曰 - 高公 refers to the magistrate, 大骇 means 'greatly startled,' 阁署 means 'government office,' 传视 means 'to show around,' and 曰 means 'said.'
  • 此物程朱不能格也 - 此物 means 'this thing,' 程朱 refers to Cheng and Zhu, two prominent Neo-Confucian philosophers, 不能 means 'cannot,' and 格 means 'influence' or 'control.'
  • 凡物岁久则为妖 - 凡物 means 'any object,' 岁久 means 'after many years,' 则 means 'then,' and 为妖 means 'becomes a demon.'
  • 得人精气多，亦能为妖 - 得 means 'acquires,' 人精气 means 'human essence or energy,' 多 means 'a lot,' 亦能 means 'also can,' and 为妖 means 'become a demon.'
  • 众议碎之，犹豫未决 - 众议 means 'the crowd discussed,' 碎之 means 'to break it,' 犹豫 means 'hesitated,' and 未决 means 'not decided.'
  • 次日仍屈还故形 - 次日 means 'the next day,' 仍 means 'still,' 屈 means 'bent,' 还 means 'back,' and 故形 means 'original shape.'
  • 投炽炉中，似微有呦呦声 - 投 means 'throw,' 炽炉 means 'hot furnace,' 似 means 'seemed,' 微 means 'slightly,' 有 means 'had,' and 呦呦声 means 'a faint sound.'
  • 高氏自此渐式微 - 高氏 means 'the Gao family,' 自此 means 'from then on,' 渐 means 'gradually,' and 式微 means 'declined.'
  • 武清王庆垞曹氏厅柱，忽生牡丹二朵 - 武清王 is a title, 庆垞 is a place name, 曹氏 means 'the Cao family,' 厅柱 means 'hall pillar,' 忽 means 'suddenly,' 生 means 'grew,' and 牡丹 means 'peony.'
  • 瓣中脉络如金丝 - 瓣 means 'petals,' 中 means 'inside,' 脉络 means 'veins,' and 如金丝 means 'like golden threads.'
  • 花叶葳蕤 - 花叶 means 'flowers and leaves,' and 葳蕤 means 'luxuriant' or 'flourishing.'
  • 越七八日乃萎落 - 越 means 'after,' 七八日 means 'seven or eight days,' 乃 means 'then,' and 萎落 means 'wilted and fell.'
  • 其根从柱而出，纹理相连 - 其根 means 'its roots,' 从柱而出 means 'came out from the pillar,' 纹理 means 'grain,' and 相连 means 'connected.'
  • 近柱二寸许，尚是枯木 - 近柱 means 'near the pillar,' 二寸许 means 'about two inches,' 尚是 means 'still was,' and 枯木 means 'dry wood.'
  • 以上乃渐青 - 以上 means 'above,' 乃 means 'then,' and 渐青 means 'gradually turned green.'
  • 先太夫人，曹氏甥也 - 先太夫人 means 'my late mother,' 曹氏 means 'the Cao family,' and 甥 means 'nephew.'
  • 小时亲见之 - 小时 means 'when young,' 亲见 means 'personally saw,' and 之 means 'it.'
  • 咸曰瑞也 - 咸 means 'all,' 曰 means 'said,' and 瑞 means 'auspicious sign.'
  • 外祖雪峰先生曰 - 外祖 means 'maternal grandfather,' 雪峰 is a name, and 先生 means 'sir' or 'teacher.'
  • 物之反常者为妖，何瑞之有！ - 物之反常者 means 'things that behave abnormally,' 为妖 means 'become demons,' and 何瑞之有 means 'what auspicious signs are there!'
  • 曹氏亦式微 - 曹氏 means 'the Cao family,' and 亦式微 means 'also declined.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 高宜人 - A title given to a woman of noble status, indicating her high social standing.
  • 程朱 - Refers to Cheng Yi and Zhu Xi, two prominent Neo-Confucian philosophers known for their influential works in Chinese philosophy.
  • 牡丹 - Peonies are often associated with wealth and honor in Chinese culture and are considered auspicious flowers.
  • 雪峰先生 - Likely a reference to a respected elder or scholar, possibly a family friend or relative.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '先叔母高宜人之父，讳荣祉，官山西陵川令' introduces the main subject and provides background information about his position and family.
  • The sentence '有一旧玉马，质理不甚白洁，而血浸斑斑，斫紫檀为座承之' describes the jade horse, its condition, and how it was displayed.
  • The sentence '恒置几上，其前足本为双跪欲起之形，一日左足忽伸出于座外' describes the horse's initial appearance and a sudden change in its posture.
  • The sentence '高公大骇，阁署传视曰：此物程朱不能格也' shows the magistrate's reaction and the belief that even great scholars could not control the horse.
  • The sentence '凡物岁久则为妖，得人精气多，亦能为妖' explains the belief that objects can become demons after a long time and by absorbing human energy.
  • The sentence '众议碎之，犹豫未决' describes the indecision among the people regarding what to do with the horse.
  • The sentence '次日仍屈还故形' indicates that the horse returned to its original shape the next day.
  • The sentence '投炽炉中，似微有呦呦声' describes the attempt to destroy the horse and the faint sound it made.
  • The sentence '高氏自此渐式微' marks the decline of the Gao family after this incident.
  • The sentence '武清王庆垞曹氏厅柱，忽生牡丹二朵' introduces a new event involving the Cao family and their hall pillar.
  • The sentence '瓣中脉络如金丝，花叶葳蕤' describes the beauty of the peonies that grew on the pillar.
  • The sentence '越七八日乃萎落，其根从柱而出，纹理相连，近柱二寸许，尚是枯木，以上乃渐青' describes the peonies' growth and the gradual change in the wood.
  • The sentence '先太夫人，曹氏甥也，小时亲见之' provides personal testimony from the narrator's mother about the event.
  • The sentence '咸曰瑞也，外祖雪峰先生曰：物之反常者为妖，何瑞之有！' shows the differing opinions on whether the peonies were auspicious or demonic.
  • The sentence '曹氏亦式微' marks the decline of the Cao family after the peony incident.

  • 先是其母梦是奴为人执缚俎上 - '先是' means 'previously,' '其母' means 'his mother,' '梦' means 'dreamed,' '是奴' means 'this slave,' '为人执缚' means 'was tied up by someone,' and '俎上' means 'on the chopping block.' This phrase describes the mother's dream where the slave was tied up on a chopping block.
  • 屠割如羊豕 - '屠割' means 'butchered,' and '如羊豕' means 'like a sheep or pig.' This phrase describes the slave being butchered like an animal.
  • 似尚有余恨 - '似' means 'seemed,' '尚' means 'still,' '有余恨' means 'had lingering resentment.' This phrase describes the slave having lingering resentment.
  • 醒而恶之 - '醒' means 'awake,' '而' is a conjunction, and '恶之' means 'hated it.' This phrase describes the mother hating the dream upon waking.
  • 恒戒以毋与人斗 - '恒' means 'always,' '戒' means 'warn,' '以' is a preposition, and '毋与人斗' means 'not to fight with people.' This phrase describes the mother always warning the slave not to fight with people.
  • 不虞乃为鱼所击 - '不虞' means 'did not expect,' '乃' means 'then,' '为鱼所击' means 'to be struck by a fish.' This phrase describes the unexpected event of the slave being struck by a fish.
  • 佛氏所谓夙生中负彼命耶 - '佛氏' refers to Buddhism, '所谓' means 'what is called,' '夙生' means 'past life,' '中' means 'in,' '负彼命' means 'owed his life to,' and '耶' is a question particle. This phrase questions whether the event was due to a past life debt according to Buddhist belief.
  • 刘少宗伯青垣言 - '刘少宗伯' is a title, likely referring to a high-ranking official named Liu, and '青垣' is a place name. '言' means 'said.' This phrase introduces the speaker.

  • 系关帝祠周将军足上 - '系' means 'tied,' '关帝祠' means 'Guandi Temple,' '周将军' means 'General Zhou,' and '足上' means 'on his feet.' This phrase describes tying the colored silk on General Zhou's feet at Guandi Temple.

  • 后复密会 - '后' means 'later,' '复' means 'again,' '密会' means 'secret meeting.' This phrase describes a later secret meeting.
  • 忽见周将军击其腰 - '忽' means 'suddenly,' '见' means 'saw,' '周将军' means 'General Zhou,' '击' means 'hit,' and '其腰' means 'his waist.' This phrase describes suddenly seeing General Zhou hitting his waist.

  • 夫专其利而移祸于人 - '夫' is a particle, '专其利' means 'to monopolize the benefit,' '而' is a conjunction, '移祸于人' means 'to shift the disaster onto others.' This phrase describes monopolizing the benefit and shifting the disaster onto others.

  • 有一鬼，首大如瓮 - There is a ghost, with a head as large as a jar – a vivid and idiomatic description of a ghostly figure.
  • 舞且呼曰：來來 - Dancing and shouting, 'Come, come' – a compound verb phrase indicating simultaneous actions.

  • 偶相戏，我去后慎勿詈我，詈则我复来 - It was just a bit of fun; after I leave, be careful not to curse me, for if you do, I will return – a conditional statement with a warning.
  • 鸡笼是前村某家物，附汝还之 - The chicken cage belongs to a certain family in the previous village; please deliver it back to them – a formal yet conversational way of giving an instruction.
  • 语讫，以鸡笼掷车上去 - After speaking, he threw the chicken cage onto the cart – a narrative phrase describing the ghost’s action.
  • 天曙抵家 - At dawn, they arrived home – a time and event marker.
  • 夫妇并昏昏如醉 - Both husband and wife were dazed, as if drunk – a metaphorical description of confusion or disorientation.

  • 刘四亦流落无人状 - Liu Si also ended up in a state of destitution, without anyone – a metaphorical description of his fate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 奴子 - A term used by a servant or lower-status person to refer to themselves, indicating a social hierarchy.
  • 乞假归省 - A common practice in classical Chinese society, where officials or servants would request time off to visit their families.
  • 御牛车 - A means of transport used by commoners; ox carts were common in ancient China for travel and transport.
  • 鬼 - A supernatural being in Chinese folklore, often depicted as mischievous or vengeful, especially when disturbed or offended.
  • 瓮 - A large jar or ceramic vessel, used for storing food or water. Here used as a simile for the size of the ghost’s head.
  • 鸡笼 - A cage for chickens, commonly used in rural households. The ghost’s use of it suggests a connection to the village.
  • 前村某家 - A certain family in the previous village – a common way to refer to a specific household without naming them, which may indicate a desire to avoid conflict or a lack of knowledge.
  • 詈 - To curse or scold; a term used in classical Chinese for verbal abuse, often with supernatural consequences.
  • 流落无人状 - A description of a person who has fallen into destitution, abandoned by society or family, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 衰气 - A term referring to a weakened or diminished state of qi (vital energy), often used in traditional Chinese medicine and supernatural contexts to explain vulnerability to ghosts or spirits.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘四 - A personal name, likely a servant or lower-status individual.
  • 壬辰 - A year in the traditional Chinese sexagenary cycle. '壬辰' corresponds to the year 1442 in the Gregorian calendar (but the exact year may vary depending on the dynasty).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先姚安公言 - A common way to introduce a story or anecdote from a respected ancestor.
  • 雍正庚戍会试 - Imperial examination (huishi) in the year Gengshen of the Yongzheng era.
  • 同号舍 - Shared the same examination room or were in the same examination booth.
  • 披发女鬼 - A woman ghost with disheveled hair; a common supernatural image in Chinese folklore.
  • 搴帘手裂其卷 - Pulled the curtain and with her hands tore his scroll; the verb 裂 (to tear) and 卷 (scroll) are key.
  • 如蛱蝶乱飞 - Like a fluttering swallowtail butterfly; a vivid metaphor for the chaotic movement of the torn paper.
  • 坐而问之曰 - Sat and asked her; a formal, respectful way to address a ghost.
  • 前生吾不知，今生则实无害人事 - I do not know about my past life, but in this life I have truly not harmed anyone; a standard form of self-justification.
  • 君非四十七号耶 - Are you not number forty-seven? A question confirming the ghost's mistaken identification.
  • 盖有二空舍，鬼除之未数也 - There were two empty rooms, and the ghost had not counted them; the ghost had not accounted for the two unused rooms.
  • 谛视良久，作礼谢罪而去 - After looking carefully for a long time, she made a bow, apologized, and left; a polite and formal resolution.
  • 斯须间 - In a short while or soon after.
  • 喧呼某甲中恶矣 - Someone shouted that Mr. Jia has been afflicted with a sudden illness; 中恶 is a classical term for sudden illness, often attributed to supernatural causes.
  • 此鬼殊愦愦 - This ghost was very confused or quite dull-witted; 愦愦 can mean confused, foolish, or dull.
  • 无妄之灾 - A calamity without cause or a misfortune without reason; a standard phrase in classical Chinese.
  • 心无愧怍 - A clear conscience or no guilt; 愧怍 means guilt or shame.
  • 仓卒间 - In haste or in a sudden moment.
  • 仅裂一卷耳 - Only one scroll was torn, that's all.
  • 否亦殆哉 - Otherwise, it would have been very dangerous; a conditional statement emphasizing the nearness of disaster.
  • 顾员外德懋 - Mr. Gu, the magistrate Gu Demao; 员外 is a title of a scholar-official or a magistrate.
  • 自言为东岳冥官 - He said he was a spirit official of the East Mountain (Dongyue); 东岳 refers to Mount Tai, which in Daoist cosmology is the seat of the god of the underworld.
  • 冥司重贞妇 - The underworld highly values chaste women; 冥司 is the underworld or the realm of the dead.
  • 有所系恋而弗去者，下也 - Those who are attached to their children, property, etc., and thus do not leave – this is the lowest level.
  • 不免情欲之萌，而能以礼义自克者，次也 - Those who cannot avoid the stirrings of desire but can restrain themselves with propriety and righteousness – this is the next level.
  • 心如枯井，波澜不生 - A heart like a dry well, with no ripples; a metaphor for emotional stillness and detachment.
  • 富贵亦不睹，饥寒亦不知，利害亦不计者，斯为上矣 - Those who do not see wealth and honor, do not know hunger and cold, and do not calculate gain and loss – these are the highest.
  • 得一则鬼神为起敬 - If one such person is found, ghosts and spirits will show reverence.
  • 喧传节妇至 - It was widely announced that a chaste wife had arrived.
  • 冥王改容 - The King of the Underworld changed his expression or looked up in reverence.
  • 冥官皆振衣伫迓 - The spirit officials all stood up and waited to receive her; 振衣 is to raise one's robe in a gesture of respect.
  • 儡然来 - Came in a puppet-like manner or lifeless, puppet-like fashion; 偶然 can also mean wooden or lifeless.
  • 其行步步渐高，如蹑阶级 - Her steps gradually rose, as if climbing a staircase; a metaphor for spiritual elevation or supernatural ascension.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 雍正庚戍 - The year Gengshen (庚申) in the reign of the Yongzheng Emperor (1722–1735); corresponds to 1720.
  • 会试 - Imperial Examination (huishi), the second stage of the imperial examination system, held in the capital.
  • 号舍 - Examination rooms in the imperial examination halls, assigned to candidates.
  • 女鬼 - Female ghosts, common in Chinese folklore and often depicted as vengeful or supernatural.
  • 中恶 - A term for sudden illness or affliction, often attributed to supernatural causes or curses.
  • 无妄之灾 - A classical expression for a calamity that happens without cause or warning.
  • 东岳冥官 - An official of the Underworld, specifically associated with Mount Tai (东岳), which in Daoist cosmology is the domain of the King of the Underworld (Dongyue Daxian).
  • 贞妇 - A chaste wife or virtuous woman, a highly valued moral ideal in Confucian and Daoist thought.
  • 冥司 - The underworld or the spirit realm, where the souls of the dead are judged.
  • 节妇 - A virtuous widow or chaste woman, a term used to denote moral excellence in women.
  • 冥王 - The King of the Underworld, the supreme judge of the afterlife.
  • 冥官 - Officials of the underworld, who serve the King of the Underworld in judging the dead.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 号呼扑救 - Means 'shouting and trying to save themselves,' depicting the chaotic scene.
  • 圣贤乃谋匿人财耶 - Means 'are the sages plotting to hide someone's money,' a sarcastic remark by the monk.
  • 合掌向塾师曰 - Means 'clasped his hands and said to the teacher,' indicating a respectful gesture.
  • 异端偶触忤圣贤 - Means 'heretical person occasionally offended the sages,' a polite way of acknowledging the teacher's authority.
  • 幸见恕 - Means 'please forgive me,' a request for leniency.

  • 见僧辄诋 - Means 'he always criticized the monk,' suggesting a pattern of behavior.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence introduces the narrator and sets the scene, but the relationship between the narrator and the events is not immediately clear.
  • The sequence of events is clear, but the monk's persistence and the teacher's reaction are nuanced.
  • The teacher's harsh words and actions are vividly described, but the underlying tension between the two is complex.
  • The monk's response is a sophisticated argument that draws a parallel between his actions and those of Confucian scholars.
  • The teacher's reaction and the monk's departure are described in a series of quick, dramatic actions.
  • The teacher's expectation and the subsequent discovery of the bees are described in a suspenseful manner.
  • The teacher's cautious approach and the need for clarity are emphasized.
  • The sudden and chaotic event is vividly described.
  • The monk's unexpected return and his sarcastic remark add a layer of irony to the story.
  • The monk's respectful gesture and the spectators' reaction provide a final, humorous note.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 朱青雷言 - A literary device where '朱青雷' (Zhu Qinglei) is the name of the narrator or storyteller, often used to introduce a tale or anecdote.
  • 避仇 - Means 'to avoid enemies' or 'to evade conflict.'
  • 窜匿深山 - Means 'to hide in a deep mountain,' indicating a place of refuge.
  • 时月白风清 - A poetic description of a clear and quiet night.
  • 徙倚白杨下 - Means 'to linger under a white poplar tree,' suggesting a ghostly presence.
  • 伏不敢起 - Means 'to crouch and dare not stand up,' indicating fear.
  • 栗而答曰 - Means 'trembling and replied,' showing the person's fear.
  • 至可畏者莫若人 - Means 'the most terrifying thing is a person,' emphasizing the power of human actions.
  • 使君颠沛至此者，人耶鬼耶？一矣而隐 - A rhetorical question asking whether the person's misfortune is due to humans or ghosts, with the implication that both are the same in causing harm.
  • 余谓此青雷有激之寓言也 - Means 'I believe this is Zhu Qinglei's allegorical story,' indicating that the story has a deeper meaning.
  • 都察院库中有巨蟒 - Means 'there is a huge python in the treasury of the Censorate,' referring to a government institution.
  • 时或夜出 - Means 'sometimes it comes out at night,' indicating the python's nocturnal activity.
  • 蟠迹著尘处，约广二寸余 - Means 'the footprint of the python in the dust is about two inches wide,' describing the size of the footprint.
  • 计其身当横径五寸 - Means 'estimating its body, it should be about five inches in diameter,' giving an idea of the python's size.
  • 壁无罅，门亦无罅，窗棱阔不及二寸 - Means 'the wall has no cracks, the door has no gaps, and the window frames are less than two inches wide,' describing the tight security.
  • 不识何以出入 - Means 'it is unknown how it enters and exits,' posing a mystery.
  • 大抵物久则能化形 - Means 'generally speaking, things that exist for a long time can change their form,' suggesting a magical or supernatural ability.
  • 狐魅能自窗隙往来，其本形亦非窗隙所容也 - Means 'fox spirits can come and go through window gaps, and their original form cannot fit through those gaps,' further emphasizing the supernatural ability.
  • 堂吏云其出应休咎，殊无验 - Means 'the officials in the hall say that its appearance foretells good or bad luck, but it has never been verified,' indicating a superstitious belief.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 幽明异路 - 幽 refers to the mysterious or hidden realm, 明 to the visible or human world, and 异路 means different paths. This phrase contrasts the realms of the living and the dead.
  • 人所能治者，鬼神不必更治之 - 治 means to govern or manage. This phrase suggests that if humans can handle something, the gods or spirits do not need to intervene.
  • 示不渎也 - 示 means to show or indicate, and 不渎 means not disrespectful. This phrase implies that such non-intervention is a sign of respect.
  • 幽明一理 - 一理 means the same principle. This phrase suggests that the realms of the living and the dead are governed by the same underlying principle.
  • 人所不及治者，鬼神或亦代治之 - 不及治 means cannot manage, and 代治之 means to manage on their behalf. This phrase suggests that when humans cannot handle something, the gods or spirits may step in.
  • 示不测也 - 不测 means unpredictable. This phrase implies that such intervention is a sign of the gods' mysterious and unpredictable nature.
  • 度事不可止，乃密约同逃 - 度事不可止 means considered the matter unstoppable, and 密约同逃 means secretly agreed to escape together. This phrase describes a situation where the matter is deemed unstoppable, leading to a secret agreement to escape together.

Proper Nouns
