Idiomatic Expressions

  • 肃宁去京四百余里 - A locative phrase indicating the distance from Sùnìng to the capital (Beijing), used to establish the geographical context.
  • 比信返，天甫微明 - A time-related expression meaning 'by the time the messenger returned, the sky was just beginning to brighten.' The phrase uses classical syntax to convey a precise moment in time.
  • 演剧犹未散 - 'The play had not yet ended.' This is a common expression in classical Chinese to denote the ongoing nature of a performance.
  • 匆匆束装 - 'Hastily packed one’s belongings.' This phrase conveys urgency and haste.
  • 官吏迎候者已供帐相属 - 'Officials who had come to greet him were already lined up with accommodations prepared.' The phrase uses a classical structure to describe a chain of officials waiting with prepared lodgings.
  • 父子俱称疾不出 - 'Father and son both claimed illness and did not go out.' This is a common expression in classical Chinese to indicate avoidance of public appearances or political involvement.
  • 是岁为天启甲子 - 'This year was the first year of the Tianqi era (Jiazi).' This is a standard way of dating in classical Chinese.
  • 越三载而忠贤败，竟免于难 - 'After three years, Zhongxian fell, and he was finally saved from calamity.' The phrase uses a classical structure to describe a delayed outcome.
  • 岌乎危哉 - An exclamatory expression meaning 'How perilous it was!' or 'How dangerous it was!' Used to emphasize the nearness of danger.
  • 濒行时 - 'At the time of departure.' A common phrase used to mark a moment before a significant event.
  • 先使新妇识我面 - 'Let her first wife recognize my face.' This is a formal and somewhat archaic expression used in a context of power and control.
  • 貌修伟而秀削 - 'His appearance was tall and vigorous, yet refined and slender.' This is a classical way of describing physical features.
  • 色隐赤 - 'His complexion was slightly reddish.' This is a description of skin tone.
  • 两颧微露 - 'His cheekbones were slightly visible.' This is a classical way of describing facial structure.
  • 颊微狭 - 'His cheeks were slightly narrow.' Another classical description of facial features.
  • 目光如醉 - 'His eyes were as if drunk.' This is an idiomatic expression used to describe a dazed or intense gaze.
  • 卧蚕以上，赭石薄晕，如微肿 - A detailed description of the face, particularly around the eyes. '卧蚕' refers to the area under the eyes; '赭石' is a reddish mineral, used here metaphorically to describe a faint reddish hue; '薄晕' means a light shade or tint; '如微肿' suggests a slight swelling or puffiness.
  • 衣绯红 - 'Wore a crimson robe.' '绯红' is a classical term for a deep red color.
  • 座旁几上，露列金印九 - 'On the table beside him, nine golden seals were displayed in plain sight.' This is a classical way of describing the display of power or authority.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 肃宁 - A place name, likely referring to Sùnìng, a county in Hebei Province, China. It is used here to establish the geographical setting of the narrative.
  • 京 - The capital city, most likely Beijing during the Ming Dynasty.
  • 天启甲子 - Refers to the first year of the Tianqi era, which is 1624. This is a standard way of dating in classical Chinese.
  • 忠贤 - Refers to Wei Zhongxian, the powerful eunuch during the reign of the Tianqi Emperor (Zhongxian was a key figure in the Ming court and was later executed).
  • 于生 - A name of a character, likely a scholar or official. The text describes his escape from danger due to the downfall of Wei Zhongxian.
  • 于翁 - 'The old man of the Yu family,' likely referring to the father of '于生.'
  • 余家为表戚 - 'Our family is related by marriage.' This indicates a familial connection between the narrator and the Yu family.
  • 余儿时尚见此轴 - 'When I was still a child, I saw this painting.' This indicates that the narrator is an older person recounting a past event.
  • 金印九 - 'Nine golden seals.' In the Ming Dynasty, seals (yin) were symbols of official rank and authority. The number nine is significant in Chinese culture, often associated with imperial power.
  • 绯红 - A deep red color, often associated with nobility and high rank in classical Chinese contexts.

  • 後風清月白之夕 - Poetic phrase meaning 'on nights when the wind was clear and the moon bright,' often used to set a serene or supernatural atmosphere.
  • 或笑之，則隱 - 'If someone laughed at him, he would disappear,' indicating the ghost's reaction to mockery.
  • 泥古者愚，何愚乃至是欤 - 'Those who cling to antiquity are foolish, how foolish can one be to reach this point?' a critical comment on rigid adherence to tradition.
  • 神而明之，存乎其人 - Classical phrase from the *Book of Changes* (Yijing), meaning 'To make the divine clear and manifest depends on the person.'
  • 能與人規矩，不能使人巧 - Quote from the *Book of Rites* (Liji), meaning 'One can teach people the rules, but not the skill or ingenuity.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阿文勤公 - A title or honorific, likely referring to a historical figure, possibly A Wen Qin Gong, which may be the honorific title of Aixinjueluo Wen Qin, a Qing dynasty official. Rendered as Lord Wen Qin or A Wen Qin Gong.
  • 昀 - The name Yun (昀), likely referring to Ji Yun (紀昀), a famous Qing dynasty scholar and compiler of the *Four Treasuries* (Siku Quanshu), also known as Master Ji or Ji Yün.
  • 國弈不废旧谱，而不执旧谱 - Refers to the game of Go (Weiqi), indicating that while masters do not discard old strategies, they do not rigidly adhere to them.
  • 國醫不泥古方，而不離古方 - Refers to traditional Chinese medicine, emphasizing flexibility in using ancient prescriptions without being bound by them.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿文勤公 - Likely refers to A Wen Qin Gong (阿文勤公), possibly a Qing dynasty official. As the name is uncertain, it should be rendered as Lord Wen Qin or A Wen Qin Gong.
  • 昀 - The name Yun (昀), likely referring to Ji Yun (紀昀), a famous Qing dynasty scholar and compiler of the *Four Treasuries*. He is also known as Ji Yün or Master Ji.
  • 國弈 - Refers to the national or traditional game of Go (Weiqi).
  • 國醫 - Refers to the traditional Chinese medical system, or the 'national medicine' of China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 史册所未睹也 - Unprecedented in history, indicating that Wei Zhongxian's evil was unmatched.
  • 或言其知事必败 - Some say he knew his downfall was certain, a common way to express a rumor or belief.
  • 阴蓄一骡，日行七百里 - Secretly kept a mule that could travel seven hundred miles a day, an exaggerated and metaphorical expression.
  • 以备逋逃 - To prepare for fleeing, indicating a plan for escape.
  • 阴蓄一貌类己者以备代死 - Secretly kept someone who looked like him to prepare for his death, another exaggerated and metaphorical expression.
  • 后在阜城尤家店，竟用是私遁去 - Later, in Fucheng's Youjiadou, he indeed used this to secretly flee, a narrative statement about the event.
  • 余谓此无稽之谈也 - I believe this is groundless talk, an idiomatic expression indicating skepticism.
  • 以天道论之 - Considering the way of heaven, a philosophical and moral consideration.
  • 苟神理不诬 - If divine principles are not false, a conditional statement about divine justice.
  • 忠贤断无幸免理 - Wei Zhongxian could not avoid punishment, a logical conclusion based on divine principles.
  • 以人事论之 - Considering human affairs, a practical and worldly consideration.
  • 擅政七年 - Governing for seven years, a straightforward statement about his tenure.
  • 何人不识 - Who does not know him? a rhetorical question emphasizing his widespread recognition.
  • 使窜伏旧党之家 - If he hid in the homes of his old party members, a hypothetical scenario.
  • 小人之交，势败则离 - Friends among the unworthy, when power fails, they separate, a common saying about the nature of certain relationships.
  • 有缚献而已矣 - They would simply bind and surrender him, a straightforward statement about the likely outcome.
  • 使潜匿荒僻之地 - If he hid in remote and desolate places, another hypothetical scenario.
  • 耕牧之中，突来阉宦，异言异貌，骇视惊听 - Among those who farm and herd, suddenly an eunuch appears with strange words and appearance, shocking the eyes and ears, a vivid and metaphorical description.
  • 不三日必败 - Within three days, he would surely fail, a prediction based on the scenario.
  • 使远遁于封域之外 - If he fled far beyond the borders, another hypothetical scenario.
  • 严世蕃尝通日本，仇鸾尝交谙达 - Yan Shifan once communicated with Japan, and Chou Luan once associated with the Altan Khan, historical references to past events.
  • 忠贤无是也 - Wei Zhongxian did not do so, a straightforward statement about his actions.
  • 山海阻深，关津隔绝 - Mountains and seas are deep and obstructing, and customs barriers are isolated, a description of geographical obstacles.
  • 去又将何往 - Where could he go from there? a rhetorical question about the impossibility of escape.

  • 谙达 - The Altan Khan, a Mongol leader who had interactions with the Ming dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恒见小人高尺许 - Classical expression meaning 'often sees small people about a foot tall.' '小人' here is descriptive, not pejorative, referring to tiny human-like beings.
  • 男女老幼一一皆备 - Literally 'men, women, old and young, all present in full,' indicating a complete community of these small people.
  • 折柳盘为小圈，著顶上 - To bend willow branches into small rings and place them on top of the head. This is a specific ritual or custom.
  • 作队跃舞，音呦呦如度曲 - They dance in groups, their sounds like a melody being performed. '呦呦' is onomatopoeic, suggesting a soft, melodic sound.
  • 或至行帐窃食，为人所掩，则跪而泣 - Sometimes they come to the tent to steal food; if caught, they kneel and weep. This shows their behavior and reaction to being discovered.
  • 系之，则不食而死；纵之，初不敢遽行，行数尺辄回顾 - If bound, they starve to death; if released, they at first dare not move quickly, and after moving a few feet, they look back. This is a vivid description of their behavior when freed.
  • 或追叱之，仍跪泣 - If someone chases them and scolds them, they still kneel and weep. This shows their submissiveness.
  • 去人稍远，度不能追，始蓦涧越山去 - Once they are a bit far away, thinking they cannot be caught, they suddenly leap across ravines and over mountains. This is a dynamic description of their escape.
  • 然其巢穴栖止处终不可得 - Yet their nests or places of dwelling remain forever untraceable. This indicates their elusive nature.
  • 非木魅亦非山兽，盖僬侥之属 - They are neither forest spirits nor mountain beasts, but rather belong to the *Jiao Jiao* (a race of small people).
  • 不知其名，以形似小儿，而喜戴红柳，因呼曰红柳娃 - Not knowing their name, because they look like children and like to wear red willow rings, they are called *Hong Liu Wa*. This is a folk name derived from their appearance and behavior.
  • 邱县丞天锦，因巡视牧厂，曾得其一，腊以归 - County Magistrate Qiu Tianjin, during his inspection of the pastures, once captured one of them and preserved it for return. '腊以归' may mean 'preserved it in salt and brought it back' or 'dried it and brought it back.'
  • 细视其须眉毛发，与人无二 - Carefully examining its eyebrows, hair, and beard, they were no different from humans. This suggests their human-like appearance.
  • 知山海经所谓靖人，凿然有之 - This confirms the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas)’s mention of *Jing Ren* (peaceful people), which is indeed real. '凿然' means 'clearly, definitely.'
  • 有极小必有极大，列子所谓龙伯之国，亦凿然有之 - Where there is the extremely small, there must be the extremely large; the *Lie Zi* (Texts of Lie Yukou)’s mention of the *Long Bo* (Giant) country is also clearly real. This is a reference to the myth of giants in ancient Chinese texts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乌鲁木齐 - A modern place name in the Xinjiang region of China. It is likely used here as a geographical reference, though the text may be anachronistic or fictional.
  • 红柳 - 'Red willow,' a type of willow tree often found in northern China, used here as a symbolic or ceremonial element.
  • 行帐 - 'Moving tent' or 'nomadic camp,' indicating the mobile nature of the herders.
  • 邱县丞天锦 - Proper name of a local official, likely fictional or based on a historical figure.
  • 山海经 - The *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text containing mythical creatures, geography, and folklore.
  • 靖人 - A term from the *Shan Hai Jing* referring to a race of small, peaceful people. The text here seems to confirm their existence.
  • 列子 - The *Texts of Lie Yukou*, an ancient Chinese text containing fables, philosophical discussions, and mythical tales.
  • 龙伯之国 - 'The Kingdom of Long Bo,' referring to a legendary land of giants mentioned in the *Lie Zi*.

Proper Nouns

  • 乌鲁木齐 - Modern place name; rendered as 'Urumqi.'
  • 邱县丞天锦 - Proper name of a local official; rendered as 'County Magistrate Qiu Tianjin.'
  • 山海经 - Proper title of a classical text; rendered as *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.
  • 靖人 - Term from the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*; rendered as 'Jing Ren.'
  • 列子 - Proper title of a classical text; rendered as *Texts of Lie Yukou*.
  • 龙伯之国 - Proper term from the *Texts of Lie Yukou*; rendered as 'the Kingdom of Long Bo.'
  • 红柳娃 - A folk name for the small beings; rendered as 'Hong Liu Wa' or 'Red Willow Child' with explanation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 何励庵先生言 - Formula for introducing a narrative or anecdote; 'What Mr. He Lian'an said' or 'According to Mr. He Lian'an.'
  • 明季 - Refers to the final decades of the Ming dynasty; 'Late Ming dynasty.'
  • 书生 - Refers to a young, unworldly scholar; 'A scholar' or 'a literatus.'
  • 丛莽间 - Refers to a remote and wild area; 'In the dense wilderness' or 'in the thickets.'
  • 书声琅琅 - Describes the audible presence of someone reading aloud; 'The sound of books being read clearly.'
  • 怪旷野那得有是 - Expression of surprise or disbelief; 'He wondered how there could be such a thing in the wilderness.'
  • 寻之 - Action of investigating further; 'To search for it' or 'to look for it.'
  • 老翁坐墟墓间 - Describes a scene of solitude and decay; 'An old man sitting in a desolate graveyard.'
  • 各捧书蹲坐 - Describes the foxes' posture and activity; 'Each holding a book and squatting.'
  • 既解读书 - Indicates a process of learning; 'Once they understand reading.'
  • 必不为祸 - Expresses a belief in the benign nature of the foxes; 'They will certainly not cause harm.'
  • 因与揖让 - Indicates a polite and respectful interaction; 'He then bowed and gestured to invite them.'
  • 席地坐 - Describes a simple and informal seating arrangement; 'They sat on the ground.'
  • 问读书何为 - Initiates a dialogue; 'He asked what the purpose of reading was.'
  • 吾辈皆修仙者也 - Indicates a shared goal of spiritual advancement; 'We are all those who cultivate immortality.'
  • 凡狐之求仙有二途 - Sets up a dichotomy; 'There are two paths for foxes seeking immortality.'
  • 其一采精气，拜星斗，渐至通灵变化，然后积修正果，是为由妖而求仙 - Describes a more direct but risky path; 'One path involves gathering vital energy, worshipping stars, gradually becoming spiritual and changing form, and then accumulating good deeds to achieve immortality, which is the path from demon to immortality.'
  • 然或入邪僻，则干天律，其途捷而危 - Warns of the dangers of this path; 'However, if one strays into evil, it violates heavenly laws, making this path quick but dangerous.'
  • 其一先炼形为人，既得为人，然后讲习内丹，是为由人而求仙 - Describes a safer but longer path; 'The other path involves first transforming one's form to become human, and once human, then practicing internal alchemy, which is the path from human to immortality.'
  • 虽吐纳导引，非旦夕之功，而久久坚持，自然圆满 - Emphasizes the importance of patience and perseverance; 'Although breathing exercises and guiding techniques are not accomplished in a day or two, with persistent effort, they naturally lead to perfection.'
  • 顾形不自变，随心而变 - Highlights the role of mental transformation; 'However, the form does not change by itself; it changes with the heart.'
  • 故先读圣贤之书，明三纲五常之理，心化则形亦化矣 - Explains the importance of moral education; 'Therefore, one should first read the books of sages and virtuous people, understand the principles of the three bonds and five constants, and when the heart changes, the form will also change.'
  • 书生借视其书，皆五经论语孝经孟子之类 - Describes the content of the books; 'The scholar asked to see the books, which were all Five Classics, Analects, Book of Filial Piety, and Mencius.'
  • 但有经文而无注 - Indicates the simplicity of the books; 'But there were only the texts of the classics without annotations.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 何励庵先生 - Refers to Mr. He Lian'an, a historical figure or a person known in the context of the story.
  • 明季 - Refers to the late Ming dynasty, a period of political turmoil and social change.
  • 书生 - Refers to a young, unworldly scholar, often depicted in literature and folklore.

  • 五经 - Refers to the Five Classics: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).
  • 论语 - Refers to the *Analects of Confucius*, a collection of sayings and ideas attributed to Confucius.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 問經不解釋，何由講貫？ - Rhetorical question asking how one can teach or discuss the classics without explanation. '何由' means 'how' or 'why', emphasizing the necessity of explanation.
  • 口相授受，疏通訓詁，即可知其義旨 - Phrase describing the process of oral transmission and textual clarification to grasp the meaning. '疏通訓詁' refers to explaining and interpreting classical texts.
  • 以講經求科第，支離敷衍，其詞愈美而經愈荒 - Critique of using the study of classics to pursue official examinations, which leads to superficial and fragmented interpretations. '支離敷衍' means 'fragmented and superficial'.
  • 以講經立門戶，紛紜辯駁，其說愈詳而經亦愈荒 - Critique of using the study of classics to establish sectarian positions, which results in excessive and contradictory arguments. '紛紜辯駁' means 'excessive and contradictory arguments'.
  • 凡巧妙之術，中間必有不穩處 - Statement suggesting that any clever or skillful method must have some inherent instability. '中間必有不穩處' means 'there must be some instability in the middle'.
  • 如步步踏實，即小有蹉失，終不至折肱傷足 - Metaphor comparing steady, grounded actions to avoiding serious mistakes, even if minor ones occur. '折肱傷足' means 'breaking the arm or injuring the foot', symbolizing serious setbacks.
  • 與所云修仙二途，亦同一意也 - Statement linking the above metaphor to the two paths of cultivating immortality, suggesting a similar principle. '修仙二途' refers to the two paths of cultivating immortality in Daoist thought.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 經 - Refers to the classical texts of Confucianism, such as the *Five Classics* (五經).
  • 聖賢 - Refers to sages and virtuous people, particularly Confucian figures like Confucius and Mencius.
  • 科第 - Refers to the imperial examination system used to select officials in ancient China.
  • 修仙二途 - Refers to the two paths of cultivating immortality in Daoist thought, often symbolizing different approaches to spiritual or intellectual pursuits.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吾虽方外闲身，不预人事 - I, though a recluse, do not interfere in worldly affairs.

  • 烦转道意，后毋相渎 - Please convey my message and do not disturb me again.
  • 君近得新果，偏食儿女，而独忘孤癟 - You recently obtained new fruits and favored your children while forgetting the orphaned and the poor.
  • 使啜泣竟夕，虽是无心，要由于意有歧视 - This caused them to weep all night. Although it was unintentional, it stemmed from a discriminatory mindset.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 娶孟夫人 - Literally means 'married Lady Meng,' referring to the marriage of the official to Lady Meng.
  • 早卒 - Means 'died young' or 'passed away early.'
  • 月夜獨坐衙齋 - Describes the official sitting alone in his office during a moonlit night.
  • 恍惚夢夫人自樹梢翩然下 - A poetic description of the official dreaming that his late wife descended gracefully from a tree branch.
  • 相勞苦如平生 - Means 'we exchange the usual greetings and concerns as if we were still together in life.'
  • 吾本天女，宿命當為君婦，緣滿乃歸 - A statement by the deceased wife indicating that she was originally a heavenly maiden, destined to be the official's wife due to their karmic connection, and now returns because her fate is complete.
  • 今過此相遇，亦余緣之未尽者也 - Means 'now we meet again, it is because our karmic connection is not yet finished.'
  • 官不止此，行去矣 - The wife predicts that the official's career will not stop at this point and that he will advance further.
  • 此難言，公卒時不在鄉里，不在官署，不在道途館驛，亦不殁于戰陣 - The wife cryptically states that the official's death will not occur in familiar places or in battle, but the exact location and circumstances are not easily predicted.
  • 時至自知耳 - Means 'when the time comes, you will know.'
  • 殁後尚相見乎 - Asks whether they will meet again after death.
  • 此在君矣，君努力生天，即可見，否則不能也 - The wife says that whether they meet again depends on the official; if he strives to reach the heavens, they will meet, otherwise they won't.
  • 征叛苗 - Refers to the official leading a campaign against rebellious Miao tribes.
  • 師還，卒于戎幕之下 - Describes the official dying on the battlefield.
  • 奴子魏藻性佻蕩，好窺伺婦女 - Describes Hui Zao, a servant, as being flirtatious and fond of spying on women.
  • 一日村外遇少女，似相識而不知其姓名居址 - Narrates Hui Zao encountering a young girl outside the village, whom he recognizes but does not know her name or residence.
  • 挑與語，女不答而目成，徑西去 - Describes Hui Zao trying to talk to the girl, but she only responds with a glance and then walks away.
  • 藻方注視，女回顧若招，即隨以往 - Hui Zao follows the girl as she looks back as if inviting him.
  • 漸逼近，女面癠小語曰：來往人眾，恐見疑 - As Hui Zao approaches, the girl whispers, expressing concern about being seen by others.
  • 君可相隔小半里，俟到家，吾待君牆外東屋中——棗樹下系一牛，旁有碌碡者是也 - The girl instructs Hui Zao to wait a little distance away until she arrives home and will meet him outside her eastern house near a tied-up ox and a stone roller.
  • 既而漸行漸遠，薄暮將抵李家洼，去家二十里矣 - The girl moves further away, and as evening approaches, she reaches Li's depression, twenty miles from her home.
  • 宿雨初晴，泥將沒脛，足趾亦腫痛 - Describes the weather clearing after rain, with mud up to the knees and swollen, painful feet.
  • 遙見女已入東屋，方竊喜，趨而赴 - Hui Zao sees the girl enter her eastern house, secretly pleased, and rushes towards it.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 齐大，献县剧盗也 - Refers to Qi Da, a notorious bandit from Xian County; '剧盗' (jù dào) means particularly bold or notorious bandit.
  • 一盗见其妇善，逼污之 - One bandit saw her wife's beauty and forced himself on her; '逼污之' (bī wū zhī) means to forcibly defile or assault.
  • 刃胁不从，反接其手缚于凳 - He threatened with a knife, but she refused to comply, and he tied her hands behind her back and bound her to a chair; '反接其手' (fǎn jiē qí shǒu) means to tie hands behind the back.
  • 已褫下衣，呼两盗左右挟其足矣 - He had already stripped her of her clothes and called two other bandits to hold her feet; '褫下衣' (chǐ xià yī) means to strip off clothes.
  • 齐大方看庄 - Qi Da was on guard; '看庄' (kàn zhuāng) is a term used by bandits to mean being on lookout duty.
  • 汹汹欲斗，目光如饿虎 - He was ready to fight fiercely, his eyes like those of a hungry tiger; '汹汹欲斗' (xiōng xiōng yù dòu) means to be fiercely ready to fight.
  • 间不容发之顷 - In an instant where there was no room for a hair; hyperbolic expression meaning an extremely short time.
  • 惟齐大终不能弋获 - Only Qi Da could not be captured; '弋获' (yì huò) means to capture or catch.
  • 群盗云官来捕时，齐大实伏马槽下 - The bandits said that when the officials came to arrest them, Qi Da was actually hiding under the horse trough; '伏马槽下' (fú mǎ cáo xià) means to hide under a horse trough.
  • 兵役皆云，往来搜数过 - The soldiers all said that they searched multiple times; '往来搜数过' (wǎng lái sōu shù guò) means to search multiple times.
  • 惟见槽下朽竹一束，约千余竿，积尘污秽，似弃置多年者 - They only saw a bundle of rotten bamboo, about a thousand or so stalks, covered in dust and filth, as if discarded many years ago; '朽竹一束' (xiǔ zhú yī shù) means a bundle of rotten bamboo.
  • 从兄懋园闻之曰 - His older brother Mao Yuan heard this and said; '从兄' (cóng xiōng) means older brother.
  • 此狐黑白太明 - This fox is too black and white; metaphorical expression meaning the fox is too straightforward or obvious.

  • 乞示谕百姓，仆妾勿凌虐，债负勿通索，诸事互让勿争斗，庶鬼无所施其技 - The old man requests the magistrate to issue an edict advising the people not to abuse servants and maids, not to demand debts, and to avoid disputes, hoping that this would prevent the ghosts from causing harm.
  • 先生震怒，笞而逐之 - The magistrate became furious and beat the old man before dismissing him.

  • 家家为备，缢而获解者果二 - People prepared themselves, and indeed two people who attempted suicide were saved.

  • 乃知数虽前定，苟能尽人力，亦必有一二之挽回 - Thus, it became clear that although fate might be predetermined, human effort could still save some lives.
  • 又知人命至重，鬼神虽前知其当死，苟一线可救，亦必转借人力以救之 - It also became known that human life is very precious, and even if spirits foresaw someone's death, they would still rely on human intervention to save them if there was any chance.
  • 盖气运所至，如严冬风雪，天地亦不得不然 - This is because the flow of events is like the harsh winter winds and snow, and the heavens and earth must also follow this course.

  • 六合之外，圣人存而不论，阙所疑可矣 - Means that beyond the six directions (all around), sages preserve it but do not discuss it, and it is sufficient to leave the doubts unaddressed; '六合' refers to the six directions, '存而不论' means to preserve but not discuss, and '阙所疑可矣' means to leave the doubts unaddressed.

  • The sequence describes the actions of the person leading the procession, including holding a torch, standing aside, and looking at the woman.
  • This clause describes the narrator and the woman leaving and looking back, seeing the person following them to the house.
  • This clause describes the person being stopped by the gatekeeper, begging, and then being allowed to enter.
  • This clause describes the person entering, hiding in a corner, looking at the woman, performing a ritual, and standing motionless.
  • This clause describes the woman entering the room and moving closer to the window.
  • This clause describes the narrator being asked to return to see the child and following the person back.
  • This clause describes the person entering the woman's room and looking at every place where the woman sat or slept.
  • This clause describes the narrator hearing the child cry for the mother, rushing out, circling the child, and holding hands in a helpless gesture.

  • 忘其名字 - 'Forgot his name,' a common way to express that the name is unknown or unimportant.
  • 恒于场圃间对谈 - 'Often conversed in the fields and gardens,' a phrase describing a habitual activity in a natural setting.
  • 狐自称生于北宋初 - 'The fox claimed to have been born at the beginning of the Northern Song dynasty,' a specific historical reference.
  • 安叩以宋代史事 - 'An asked about historical events of the Song dynasty,' indicating a query about historical knowledge.
  • 皆不知也 - 'All of them are unknown,' a straightforward response to the query.
  • 凡学仙者，必游方之外 - 'All who learn the Dao must wander beyond the world,' a philosophical statement about the nature of Daoist practice.
  • 使万缘断绝 - 'To sever all worldly ties,' emphasizing the detachment required for spiritual cultivation.
  • 一意精修 - 'To focus solely on refined cultivation,' highlighting the intense dedication needed.
  • 如于世有所闻见 - 'If one has any knowledge or experiences in the world,' a conditional phrase.
  • 于心必有所是非 - 'One's heart will inevitably have judgments of right and wrong,' indicating the internal conflict caused by worldly knowledge.
  • 有所是非必有所爱憎 - 'If there are judgments, there will be likes and dislikes,' further elaborating on the emotional consequences.
  • 有所爱憎，则喜怒哀乐之情必迭起循生 - 'If there are likes and dislikes, emotions such as joy, anger, sorrow, and happiness will inevitably arise,' describing the emotional turmoil.
  • 以消铄其精气 - 'To consume one's vital energy,' indicating the detrimental effect of emotional disturbances.
  • 神耗而形亦敝矣 - 'The spirit is exhausted and the body is worn out,' a consequence of the energy depletion.
  • 乌能至今犹在乎 - 'How could they still exist today?' a rhetorical question emphasizing the impossibility.
  • 迨道成以后，来往人间 - 'After achieving the Dao, they come and go among people,' describing the post-achievement state.
  • 视一切机械变诈，皆如戏剧 - 'Viewing all cunning and deceit as plays,' indicating a detached perspective on worldly affairs.
  • 视一切得失胜败，以至于治乱兴亡，皆如泡影 - 'Viewing all gains and losses, victories and defeats, and even the rise and fall of dynasties as illusions,' emphasizing the transient nature of worldly phenomena.
  • 当时即不留意，又焉能一一而记之 - 'If they were not paying attention at the time, how could they remember every detail?' a logical argument.
  • 即与君相遇，是亦前缘 - 'Even meeting you is also a predestined fate,' indicating a belief in predestination.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 北宋初 - The early period of the Northern Song dynasty (960-1127), a significant era in Chinese history.
  • 学仙者 - Refers to Daoist practitioners who seek immortality or spiritual enlightenment.
  • 游方之外 - A Daoist concept referring to wandering beyond the confines of society to achieve spiritual purity.
  • 万缘断绝 - A Daoist practice of severing all worldly attachments to focus on spiritual cultivation.
  • 一意精修 - Emphasizes the intense and focused nature of spiritual practice.
  • 道成以后 - Refers to the state of having achieved the Dao, often associated with immortality or spiritual enlightenment.
  • 机械变诈 - Refers to cunning and deceitful behavior, often seen as negative in Daoist philosophy.
  • 泡影 - A metaphor for the transient and illusory nature of worldly phenomena, common in Buddhist and Daoist thought.
  • 前缘 - Refers to predestined fate or karmic connections, a concept from Buddhism and Daoism.

Proper Nouns

  • 安氏表兄 - A relative of the surname An, likely a cousin or close kin.
  • 北宋初 - The early period of the Northern Song dynasty.
  • 宋代史事 - Historical events of the Song dynasty.
  • 道成以后 - The state of having achieved the Dao.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然數百年來 - Temporal phrase meaning 'yet for hundreds of years,' introducing a reflective statement.
  • 相遇如君者不知凡幾 - Formal expression meaning 'those who meet like you are countless,' using '凡幾' to emphasize the multitude.
  • 萍水相逢，煙雲倏散 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like meeting like floating萍 (萍 is a type of floating plant), and dissipating like smoke and clouds,' describing fleeting encounters.
  • 夙昔笑言，亦多不记忆 - Phrase meaning 'the jokes and laughter of the past are mostly not remembered,' highlighting the impermanence of memories.
  • 身所未接者，从可知矣 - Logical conclusion meaning 'those I have not met can be known from this,' inferring the unknown from the known.
  • 時八里庄三官庙有雷击蝎虎一事 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'at the time, there was an incident at the Three Officials Temple in Bagli Village where a scorpion was struck by lightning.'
  • 安问以物久通灵，多撄雷斧 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how can it be that objects that have long been spiritual are often struck by lightning?'
  • 岂长生亦造物所忌乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is longevity also something the Creator abhors?'
  • 夫内丹导引，外丹服饵，皆艰难辛苦以证道 - Formal statement meaning 'internal alchemy and external elixirs, both require great effort and hardship to achieve enlightenment.'
  • 犹力田以致富，理所宜然 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like farming to become wealthy, it is naturally appropriate,' comparing spiritual cultivation to material success.
  • 若媚惑梦魇，盗采精气，损人之寿，延己之年 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'if seducing, causing nightmares, stealing vital energy, shortening others' lives, and extending one's own,' describing harmful practices.
  • 事与劫盗无异，天律不容也 - Formal statement meaning 'such actions are no different from banditry, and are not tolerated by heavenly law.'
  • 又惑恣为妖幻，贻祸生灵，天律亦不容也 - Formal statement meaning 'and if one indulges in magical illusions, causing harm to living beings, heavenly law will not tolerate this either.'
  • 若其葆养元神，自全生命，与人无患，于世无争 - Formal statement meaning 'if one preserves the original spirit, maintains life, causes no harm to others, and does not compete in the world,' describing a virtuous lifestyle.
  • 则老寿之物，正如老寿之人耳，何至犯造物之忌乎 - Formal conclusion meaning 'then a long-lived creature is just like a long-lived person, how could it offend the Creator?'
  • 舅氏实斋先生闻之曰 - Formal introduction meaning 'Uncle Mr. Shizhai heard this and said,' introducing a quote.
  • 此狐所言，皆老氏之粗浅者也 - Formal statement meaning 'what this fox says are all the basic principles of Laozi,' referring to the philosophical teachings of Laozi.
  • 然用以自养，亦足矣 - Formal conclusion meaning 'but using them for self-cultivation is sufficient,' suggesting a practical application of the teachings.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三官庙 - Refers to the Three Officials Temple, a place of worship in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 雷击蝎虎 - An incident involving a scorpion being struck by lightning, which may have cultural or mythological significance.
  • 内丹导引 - Refers to internal alchemy, a practice in Taoism aimed at achieving spiritual enlightenment.
  • 外丹服饵 - Refers to external elixirs, another practice in Taoism aimed at achieving longevity and immortality.
  • 老氏 - Refers to Laozi, the founder of Taoism, whose teachings are central to the passage.
  • 天律 - Refers to heavenly law, a concept in Chinese philosophy and religion that governs moral and ethical behavior.

Proper Nouns

  • 三官庙 - The Three Officials Temple.
  • 八里庄 - Bagli Village.
  • 实斋先生 - Uncle Mr. Shizhai.
  • 老氏 - Laozi, the founder of Taoism.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 浙江有士人 - Refers to a scholar from Zhejiang Province.
  • 夜梦至一官府 - Means 'had a dream in which he arrived at an official hall.'
  • 云都城隍庙也 - Means 'it was said to be the Temple of the City God.'
  • 有冥吏语之曰 - Means 'there was a ghost official who told him, saying.'
  • 今某公控其友负心 - Means 'now a certain official is accusing his friend of being ungrateful.'
  • 牵君为证 - Means 'and is bringing you as a witness.'
  • 君试思尝有是事否 - Means 'please think if you have ever had such a thing happen.'
  • 俄闻都城隍升坐 - Means 'soon he heard the City God ascend the throne.'
  • 冥吏白 - Means 'the ghost official reported.'
  • 某控某负心事 - Means 'that someone is accusing someone of being ungrateful.'
  • 证人已至 - Means 'the witness has arrived.'
  • 请勘断 - Means 'please investigate and judge.'
  • 都城隍举案示士人 - Means 'the City God presented a case to the scholar.'
  • 士人以实对 - Means 'the scholar answered truthfully.'
  • 此辈结党营私 - Means 'these people form cliques for their own benefit.'
  • 朋求进取 - Means 'seek advancement through alliances.'
  • 以同异为爱恶 - Means 'to regard agreement and disagreement as affection and aversion.'
  • 以爱恶为是非 - Means 'to regard affection and aversion as right and wrong.'
  • 势孤则攀附以求援 - Means 'when they are isolated, they cling to others to seek help.'
  • 力敌则排挤以互噬 - Means 'when they are equally matched, they push each other out and devour each other.'
  • 翻云覆雨 - Means 'to change one's mind as often as the weather.'
  • 倏忽万端 - Means 'in a moment, countless changes.'
  • 本为小人之交 - Means 'originally, it is the way of small-minded people to interact.'
  • 岂能责以君子之道 - Means 'how can one hold them to the standards of a gentleman?'
  • 操戈入室 - Means 'to bring weapons into the house,' implying betrayal.
  • 理所必然 - Means 'it is a natural consequence.'
  • 根勘已明 - Means 'the investigation is clear.'
  • 可驱之去 - Means 'can be driven away.'
  • 顾士人曰 - Means 'the City God looked at the scholar and said.'
  • 得无谓负心者有佚罚耶 - Means 'is it not possible that the ungrateful one will escape punishment?'
  • 夫种瓜得瓜，种豆得豆 - Means 'as you sow, so shall you reap.'
  • 因果之相偿也 - Means 'the cause and effect will compensate each other.'
  • 花既结子，子又开花 - Means 'flowers bear fruit, and the fruit bears more flowers.'
  • 因果之相生也 - Means 'the cause and effect will generate each other.'
  • 彼负心者，又有负心人蹑其后 - Means 'the ungrateful one will be followed by another ungrateful person.'
  • 不待鬼神之料理矣 - Means 'without waiting for the intervention of ghosts and spirits.'
  • 士人霍然而醒 - Means 'the scholar woke up suddenly.'
  • 后阅数载，竟如神之所言 - Means 'after several years, it turned out exactly as the god had said.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 浙江 - Refers to Zhejiang Province in China.
  • 都城隍庙 - Refers to the Temple of the City God, a place of worship for the local guardian deity.
  • 城隍 - Refers to the City God, a deity responsible for the protection and well-being of the city.
  • 冥吏 - Refers to ghost officials, beings who assist in the afterlife or during dreams.
  • 结党营私 - Refers to forming cliques for personal gain, a common criticism in Chinese literature and politics.
  • 朋求进取 - Refers to seeking advancement through alliances, another common theme in Chinese literature.
  • 操戈入室 - Refers to a person bringing weapons into the house, a metaphor for betrayal.
  • 因果 - Refers to the concept of cause and effect, a fundamental principle in Buddhism and Chinese philosophy.
  • 鬼神 - Refers to ghosts and spirits, beings that play a significant role in Chinese religious and folk beliefs.

Proper Nouns

  • 浙江 - Zhejiang Province.
  • 都城隍庙 - Temple of the City God.
  • 城隍 - City God.
  • 冥吏 - Ghost official.
  • 结党营私 - Forming cliques for personal gain.
  • 朋求进取 - Seeking advancement through alliances.
  • 操戈入室 - Bringing weapons into the house.
  • 因果 - Cause and effect.
  • 鬼神 - Ghosts and spirits.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闽中某夫人喜食猫 - A certain lady from Fujian loves to eat cats.
  • 得猫则先贮石灰于罂，投猫于内，而灌以沸汤 - Describes a method of preparing the cat: first storing lime in a jar, then placing the cat inside, and finally pouring boiling water over it.
  • 猫为灰气所蚀，毛尽脱落，不烦癷治，血尽归于脏腑，肉莹如玉 - Explains the effect of the lime on the cat: the fur falls off, the blood concentrates in the organs, and the meat becomes as clear as jade.
  • 云味胜鸡雏十倍也 - A claim that the meat tastes ten times better than young chicken.
  • 日日张网设机，所捕杀无算 - Indicates that the lady captures and kills a large number of cats daily.
  • 后夫人病危，呦呦作猫声，越十余日乃死 - The lady falls ill and makes cat-like sounds, eventually dying after about ten days.
  • 卢观察癹吉子荫文，余婿也，尝为余言之 - Introduces the narrator, who is the son-in-law of Lu Guanxian, a local official, and recounts what he heard from him.
  • 因言景州一宦家子，好取猫犬之类，拗折其足，捩之向后，观其発孑跳号以为戏，所杀亦多 - Describes another person who enjoys torturing cats and dogs for amusement, causing them to suffer before killing them.
  • 后生子女皆足躔反向前 - The children born to this person have feet that point backward, a physical deformity.
  • 又余家奴子王发，善鸟铳，所击无不中，日恒杀鸟数十，惟一子名济宁州——其往济宁州时所生也，年已十一二，忽遍体生疮，如火烙痕，每一疮内有一铁子，竟不知何由而入，百药不痊，竟以绝嗣 - Describes a servant named Wang Fa who is skilled with a bird gun, killing many birds daily. His son, named Jinan, develops a severe illness with iron particles embedded in his skin, leading to his death and the end of the family line.
  • 杀业至重，信夫 - Concludes the passage with a moral reflection on the heavy karmic consequences of killing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 闽中 - Refers to Fujian, a province in southeastern China.
  • 石灰 - Lime, a common material used in traditional Chinese cooking and medicine.
  • 沸汤 - Boiling water, used here for a specific culinary technique.
  • 癷治 - Refers to the process of cleaning and preparing meat.
  • 卢观察 - Lu Guanxian, a local official, indicating a person of some social status.
  • 景州 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region or city.
  • 宦家子 - Refers to the son of a government official, indicating a person of higher social status.
  • 鸟铳 - A type of firearm, indicating the use of modern technology in the story.
  • 济宁州 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region or city.
  • 王发 - A servant's name.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a complex process of preparing the cat, which must be read as a single, coherent action.
  • This sentence describes the effects of the lime on the cat, which must be read as a sequence of events.
  • This sentence indicates a continuous action, which must be read as a single, ongoing process.
  • This sentence describes a sequence of events leading to the lady's death.
  • This sentence describes a person's cruel behavior and its consequences.
  • This sentence describes a physical deformity in the children.
  • This sentence describes a servant's skill with a bird gun and the tragic fate of his son.
  • This sentence concludes the passage with a moral reflection.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 余嘗怪 - Formal expression meaning 'I have often wondered' or 'I have frequently found it strange.'
  • 修善果者 - Refers to those who practice good deeds or cultivate virtue.
  • 按日持齋 - Means 'to observe fasting daily according to a set schedule.'
  • 居恒 - Means 'in daily life' or 'ordinarily.'
  • 佛氏之持齋 - Refers to the Buddhist practice of fasting.
  • 菇蔬啖果 - Means 'eating vegetables and fruits.'
  • 佛大歡喜 - Means 'the Buddha is greatly pleased.'
  • 烹宰溢乎庖 - Means 'cooking and slaughtering overflow the kitchen.'
  • 肥甘羅乎俎 - Means 'delicate and fatty foods are spread on the sacrificial table.'
  • 屠割慘酷 - Means 'the act of slaughtering is cruel and harsh.'
  • 禮也 - Indicates that something is in accordance with ritual or custom.
  • 儒者遵聖賢之教 - Means 'scholars follow the teachings of the sages.'
  • 賓祭以外 - Means 'outside of sacrificial ceremonies.'
  • 時殺 - Means 'killing at certain times.'
  • 遽戕一命 - Means 'suddenly take a life.'
  • 遽戕數十命，或數百命 - Means 'suddenly take dozens or hundreds of lives.'
  • 無乃稍左乎 - Means 'isn't this somewhat inconsistent?'
  • 東坡先生 - Refers to Su Shi (Su Dongpo), a famous Song dynasty poet and scholar.
  • 酌中之道 - Means 'the middle way' or 'a balanced approach.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 佛氏 - Refers to Buddhism.
  • 齋 - Refers to fasting or abstaining from meat.
  • 準提齋期 - Refers to the fasting period dedicated to the Dizang Bodhisattva.
  • 天子無故不殺牛 - Refers to the ritual prohibition against killing oxen unless there is a specific reason.
  • 大夫無故不殺羊 - Refers to the ritual prohibition against killing sheep unless there is a specific reason.
  • 士無故不殺犬豕 - Refers to the ritual prohibition against killing dogs and pigs unless there is a specific reason.
  • 禮 - Refers to the Confucian concept of ritual propriety.
  • 儒者 - Refers to Confucian scholars.
  • 聖賢之教 - Refers to the teachings of the sages and wise men in Confucianism.
  • 東坡先生 - Refers to Su Shi, a prominent figure in Chinese literature and philosophy.

Proper Nouns

  • 東坡先生 - Su Shi (Su Dongpo), a famous Song dynasty poet and scholar.
  • 準提 - Dizang Bodhisattva, another important figure in Buddhism.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 六合以外，聖人存而不论 - Sages refrain from discussing matters beyond the six directions (六合), focusing on practical, immediate concerns.
  • 魂升魄降 - Classical concept from Chinese philosophy and religion, referring to the souls (魂) ascending and the spirits (魄) descending after death.
  • 冥司 - The underworld or the realm of the dead, often associated with Buddhist or Taoist beliefs.
  • 回煞 - Folk belief that after death, a ghost returns to the house for a short period, often associated with specific rituals and taboos.
  • 诞妄 - Means 'absurd' or 'nonsensical,' often used to criticize unfounded claims.
  • 布灰之处 - The spot where ashes or dust are scattered, often used in divination or rituals.
  • 祸福有命，死生有数 - Philosophical statement meaning that fortune and misfortune are determined by fate, and life and death are governed by destiny.
  • 蛊毒魇魅 - Refers to various forms of supernatural harm, including poisoning (蛊毒) and haunting (魇魅), often mentioned in legal texts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六合 - Traditionally refers to the six directions (north, south, east, west, above, and below), symbolizing the entirety of the physical world.
  • 魂升魄降 - Concept rooted in Chinese cosmology and religious thought, particularly in Taoism and Buddhism.
  • 冥司 - The underworld is a central theme in Chinese mythology and religious practices, often associated with Buddhist and Taoist beliefs.
  • 回煞 - Specific folk belief that involves rituals and taboos related to the return of a ghost after death.
  • 蛊毒魇魅 - Types of supernatural harm that have been documented in Chinese legal and cultural texts, reflecting the belief in the power of malevolent spirits and poisons.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase sets up a boundary for what sages discuss, implying a focus on the known and practical.
  • This clause describes the traditional belief about what happens to the soul and spirit after death.
  • This clause explains the Buddhist belief that the dead are taken to the underworld and cannot return to the living world.
  • This sentence describes a personal experience of seeing a ghostly apparition, which is a common theme in folk tales and literature.
  • This clause describes another personal experience of seeing evidence of a ghost's presence, emphasizing the eerie similarity to the deceased's appearance.
  • This philosophical statement emphasizes the idea of fate and destiny, suggesting that even the wisest cannot change it.
  • This clause introduces the concept of supernatural harm and its legal recognition in Chinese society.

[200]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 為是術者，不過瞽者巫者與土木之工 - Dismissive phrase implying that those who claim to have power over life and death are not learned scholars but common practitioners.
  • 實能禍福死生人，歷歷有驗 - Emphasizes that the effects are observable and verifiable, suggesting a supernatural or mystical power.
  • 天地鬼神之權，任其播弄無忌也 - Metaphor for how such individuals are treated as if they have the authority of divine forces.
  • 宋儒於理不可解者皆臆斷，以為無是事 - '臆斷' means 'arbitrary or speculative judgment,' and this phrase criticizes the Song Confucian scholars for dismissing phenomena they could not explain.
  • 毋乃膠柱鼓瑟乎？ - Classical idiom meaning 'is it not like forcing a fixed peg into a zither?' Criticizes inflexibility or stubborn adherence to fixed principles.
  • 郢書燕說 - Classical idiom meaning 'a letter from郢 misinterpreted in Yan.' Refers to a misunderstanding or misinterpretation of a message.
  • 君子於不知蓋阙如也 - Phrase from the *Analects of Confucius* meaning 'A gentleman, when he does not know something, covers it up and remains silent.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋儒 - Refers to Song Confucian scholars, particularly the Neo-Confucian philosophers of the 11th–13th centuries.
  • 濂洛關閩 - Refers to the four major schools of Song Neo-Confucianism: Lian (Zhou Dunyi), Lu (Cheng Yi and Cheng Hao), Guan (Zhang Zai), and Min (Zhu Xi).
  • 郭守敬 - A famous Yuan Dynasty astronomer and mathematician known for his calendar reforms and astronomical instruments.
  • 交食 - Refers to eclipses, both solar and lunar, used in ancient China for calendrical and astronomical verification.
  • 七政 - Term referring to the seven celestial bodies in ancient Chinese astronomy: the Sun, Moon, and the five visible planets.
  • 太極先天 - Refers to the Daoist and Neo-Confucian concept of 'Taiji' (Supreme Ultimate) and 'Xiantian' (before heaven), metaphysical concepts in Chinese cosmology.
  • 先聖 - Refers to ancient sages or wise men, particularly Confucius and other foundational figures in Chinese philosophy.

Proper Nouns

  • 李又聃先生 - A name of a scholar or commentator, likely a pseudonym or a fictional figure used in the text to present a critical viewpoint.
  • 郭守敬 - A real historical figure, a Yuan Dynasty astronomer and mathematician, known for his precise astronomical calculations and calendar reforms.
  • 濂洛關閩 - Refers to the four major schools of Song Confucianism.
  • 太極 - A metaphysical concept in Chinese philosophy, particularly in Neo-Confucianism and Daoism, referring to the supreme principle or origin of all things.
  • 先天 - Refers to the 'before heaven' or the primordial state, often used in conjunction with 'Taiji' in Neo-Confucian cosmology.
  • 七政 - Refers to the seven celestial bodies in ancient Chinese astronomy.
  • 先聖 - A general term for ancient sages, often used to refer to Confucius or other revered figures in Confucian tradition.
